title: Penn State men's basketball to play St. Bonaventure for preseason exhibition in Buffalo
author: Will Horstman | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State will have an exhibition game against a mid-major this upcoming season.

St. Bonaventure will host the Nittany Lions in the Greenlight Network Preseason Showcase at the KeyBank Center in Buffalo on Oct. 17, according to a news release.

The Penn State-St. Bonaventure game will be the second game of a doubleheader that also features Daemen University and St. John Fisher University. Tipoff times, ticket information and broadcast information will all be announced at a later date.

As of now, this is the only announced game that Penn State will have in the preseason. The Nittany Lions had two exhibition games last season against Dayton and Shippensburg University.

Penn State and St. Bonaventure used to be conference opponents when the Nittany Lions were in the Atlantic 10 for men's basketball before joining the Big Ten after the 1990-91 season. The two teams haven't met since they were in the same conference, and the Bonnies hold a 11-10 advantage in the all-time series.

The Nittany Lions have a former St. Bonaventure player on the roster in transfer addition Desonte Bowen, who spent the last two seasons with the Bonnies. St. Bonaventure also added Penn State transfer Mason Blackwood this offseason.

This upcoming exhibition game is being played in Buffalo because the Bonnies, who are from New York, want to build on their "strong presence" in the Buffalo market, according to the news release. Greenlight Networks is a partner with St. Bonaventure Athletics and is helping make this event happen.

title: Film review | 3-star CB Dhillon McGee offers gap control, ferocity to Penn State’s defensive backfield
author: Andrew Mercer | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State beat Texas and SMU for 3-star cornerback Dhillon McGee out of DeSoto, Texas when he announced his commitment to the Nittany Lions on Wednesday, becoming the third cornerback commit in the Class of 2027.

The 6-foot-0.5, 175-pounder is ranked as the No. 67 player out of Texas and the No. 47 cornerback in the 2027 class, per 247Sports composite. He took his official visit to Happy Valley on June 12.

In addition to playing both cornerback and wide receiver, McGee is a multi-sport athlete at DeSoto High School, playing for the school’s baseball team and running track.

On the field, he finished the 2025 season with 32 tackles, 15 pass breakups and one tackle for loss. He aided the Fighting Eagles to a 55-27 victory over C.E. King High School in the UIL 6A Division II State Championship game at AT&T Stadium.

Here’s a look at McGee’s film.

Controlling the gap

McGee expertly times his pivots when transitioning from backpedaling to running forward, ensuring that he maintains only a half-step distance behind the matched receiver. That way, when the pass is airborne, McGee doesn’t let the receiver get the separation he needs.

On man-to-man press coverage, he shows off his acceleration and long speed to keep up with the receiver’s downhill momentum. This is where his 4.4-second 40-yard dash comes into play — the initial burst of speed marks him as a threat for the entire length of the play.

He’s an incredible athlete with a blend of speed and verticality that allows him to get high and break up lob passes. With experience as a receiver, he’s used to fighting for jump balls on the offensive side of the field, so he’s able to bring that to the defense and potentially cause interceptions.

McGee’s first instinct is to have his hands on the hips and under the arms of his opponent. He loves to play rough, knocking the receiver to the wayside, but still times the attacks perfectly when the ball is descending.

McGee does this best on the sidelines where his opponents don’t have the space to break free.

Open-field physicality, tackling

McGee already possesses the height required of a collegiate cornerback, being on the taller side among Terry Smith’s current room. However, Penn State may look to have him add some muscle to give him an additional surge of power.

When the opposing team runs a screen pass or run and he breaks away from his one-on-one man coverage, McGee attacks the ball carrier with tenacity, throwing his shoulder into the opponent’s ribs and chest.

Although he doesn’t get the player on the ground, he stuns him long enough for backup to arrive. McGee and his teammates overwhelm the carrier and pile on until the whistle is blown.

Along the sidelines, McGee doesn’t need assistance. He’s proficient in steering the opponent out of bounds without giving up additional yardage after contact.

His physicality doesn’t stop at tackling. When the ball is descending from above, McGee forcefully punches or slaps down on the receivers arms right at the intended point where he’d catch the ball, causing receivers to miss catches.

Disciplined progression

In zone coverage, McGee reads the quarterback's eyes before making any decision to challenge the receiver or stay put.

If the quarterback drops back and has his eyes looking downfield, McGee will backpedal into position before engaging the receiver. However, if the quarterback faces away from his side of the field, he’ll take the next few moments to predict where the ball will go.

The tight end or running back will peel away from the offensive line for a check-down pass — which McGee has covered by coming out of the defensive backfield.

On the occasion that the quarterback scrambles outside the pocket for a throw on the run, McGee finds himself in the space between the quarterback and the receiver first before turning around to sprint toward the intended target.

The receiver is usually headed toward the sidelines for a tight catch in-bounds, but McGee can make a play on the ball due to his fluid body control and speed.

College quarterbacks have the accuracy to get the ball in the hands of uncontested receivers, and that’s where McGee’s physical aggression works best. However, if the throw is short, he’ll be there to pick it off and take it the distance the other way.

title: Breaking down the new NCAA 5-for-5 rule, implications for Penn State
author: Andrew Deal | The Daily Collegian
description:
The Division I cabinet unanimously approved a change in eligibility rules for student athletes last Tuesday.

The approved “five-for-five” rule now permits athletes competing in college for five years, rather than the previous four years they received. However, there are additional restrictions on it that’ll likely limit collegiate athletes to compete into their late 20s.

Here’s everything to know about the new rule that’ll change collegiate athletics and which Penn State players could take advantage of this.

Rule explained

The new rule states that an athlete's five years begins upon enrollment into their college or the academic year after their 19th birthday, a slight change from the initial proposal after pushback from hockey stakeholders among others.

This becomes a major change that’ll eliminate both redshirts and medical redshirts from the equation. Additionally, it prevents athletes from applying for eligibility extension waivers, something that has become prevalent in college football over the past year.

The 2026-27 season will still feature many players who hold eligibility designations beyond their senior year, but that’ll change after this season. It’ll completely go away for the 2027-28 season when the five-year rule fully goes into effect.

Those listed as seniors will get another year, but players with redshirts will have a fifth year anyways despite not playing for at least one season, so nothing will change with those players.

This rule could change how coaches and programs approach their teams and recruiting. Many players opting to return could leave less roster spots for younger players, something programs will have to work through to still operate successful teams and programs year in and year out.

Penn State players impacted

While many of the underclassmen could make use of this down the line, there’s only six players that could take advantage of it this year. Any player with a redshirt designation will have exhausted that fifth year of eligibility when their senior year comes.

Six Nittany Lions are listed as seniors, but only five of the six have a fifth year of eligibility to use while still playing in 2026, something they wouldn’t have been able to do prior to the rule change.

Quarterback Connor Barry is listed as a senior, but he was in college for four years already prior to joining Penn State. He began in 2022 with Appalachian State, before playing three years with Christopher Newport from 2023-25.

On the other hand, cornerback Zion Tracy, running back Carson Hansen, tight end Benjamin Brahmer, linebacker Kooper Ebel and safety Jamison Patton all could return to the Nittany Lions for the 2027-28 season, using that fifth year of eligibility with the new rule.

All five of those players are likely to make an impact this upcoming season, and with a chance to play for another season, the Nittany Lions could continue to build a veteran team, as many of its starters this year have been in college for multiple years already. There’s still the likelihood that some turn to the NFL after this season, but an additional option that initially wasn’t there could change some minds.

It could also affect recruiting and the transfer portal for Matt Campbell. If someone like Hansen were to return, that means all the running backs this year will be back, barring any transfers. And 4-star Aiden Gibson and 3-star Chukwuma Odoh will be added, as both will join the team next season.

Hansen’s production this season could decide whether he returns. But if he were to return, the running back room becomes even more clogged, making for some interesting decisions for the players and coaching staff.

While coaches have the opportunity to bring back some important players for another year with the new rule, it can also cause change in approach to decisions from the Penn State coaching staff and other programs around the country, depending on choices made by the players impacted by this new rule.

title: Dear Old State, tell us your secrets | A look at Penn State’s hidden lore, traditions
author: Rebecca Fein | The Daily Collegian
description:
“For the glory of Old State, for her founders strong and great, for the future that we wait, raise the song, raise the song.” Behind these words are 42,000 undergraduates, 800,000 alumni and loads of traditions.

If you’re a new student, it can be overwhelming trying to learn all the references you hear around campus. And even though a lot of it comes from experience, it’s not hard to get in the know.

Here are five important parts of Penn State’s lore everyone should know before stepping foot on campus.

Let it snow

The first tradition is an obvious one. When the first flake of snow graces campus, it's customary to grab a sled — though it’s not uncommon to substitute for lunch trays, trash bags and pool floaties instead — and rush to the HUB-Robeson Center lawn.

Students engage in snowball fights, fly down the hill in make-shift sleds and create snow angels across the lawn. Adding a bit of whimsy to the weather makes the cold more bearable.

Contrarily, it’s not uncommon to see Spikeball nets, people tanning in bathing suits and frisbees flying by when the weather hits 65 degrees. The State College weather is long and dark, so the sunlight we do receive instantly gets soaked up.

Sealing the deal

This is perhaps a lesser-known piece of lore, but it’s rumored that stepping on the seal located in the HUB-Robeson Center between the Student bookstore and Jamba Juice causes bad luck, such as failing exams or not graduating.

Since this information is not as well-known as some of the other Penn State traditions, the seal is constantly being walked on. The next time you’re in a rut, try thinking back to earlier in the day — did you step on the seal?

Good eats

D.P. Dough’s calzones in a drunken haze will forever be some students' answer to the question: “Why Penn State?”

The cheesy, saucy, doughy pocket of pizza is the standard after a night of fraternity parties and — for our 21+ readers — bar-hopping. Good thing D.P. Dough is open until 4 a.m. week-round to ensure you can get your fix any day.

If you’re not feeling pizza, no need to worry. Try Uncle Chen’s for Chinese food, Insomnia Cookies for a sweet treat, El Jefe’s Taqueria for some chips and guacamole or Raising Cane's for some good old chicken tenders.

Creamery covenant

Where else on campus would you go for an ice cream fix than the Penn State Berkey Creamery? Made with ingredients straight from Penn State’s very own dairy farm, the creamery's ice cream is a famous frozen treat.

Although it may not be known to many people, the creamery actually has three specific rules every person in the world must follow.

Number one: There is only one size. Granted, the creamery tends to give a big serving, so if you wanted a “large,” this is pretty close.

Number two: There are only blue and white sprinkles. Topping lovers beware, as the creamery only offers jimmies in the colors of Dear Old State.

Number three: There is no mixing of flavors. If you are split between two, it is better to split from the line, as the creamery prohibits mixing flavors. The only person to ever mix flavors at the creamery was former President Bill Clinton.

“We Are” cheer

Let’s finish off with one of the most well-known aspects of Penn State lore, and one you don’t have to be in Happy Valley to participate in. Whatever city, state, country or even continent you are in, chances are, whenever you hear someone shout “We Are,” it is followed by a “Penn State!” It’s nice to know we bleed blue and white all over the world.

The next time you see a Nittany Lion shirt, whether it’s on vacation, a walk down the street or in a restaurant, try screaming “We Are,” and you’ll suddenly be surrounded by Penn State family.

title: Ten hobbies to try this summer
author: Anisa Daniel-Oniko | The Daily Collegian
description:
Summer is officially in full swing. Though this freedom is temporary for students, the season still offers multiple opportunities to try something new.

Here are ten hobbies you can pick up this summer:

Reading

Picture this: picking up a beach-read to enjoy alongside sun or sand. Or maybe you’d prefer a high-octane thriller to get your heart racing. Either way, summer is as good a time as any to get into books, and reading lists can add incentive to the season.

If you’re not sure where to start with your summer reading, the New York Times recently released a list of “The 100 Best Books of the 21st Century.” These reads span a multitude of genres — including science fiction, historical fiction and fantasy.

Goodreads also has a Summer Reading Challenge, platforming books like “Never Let Me Go” by Kazuo Ishiguro and “People We Meet on Vacation” by Emily Henry.

Arts and crafts

Painting. Drawing. Pottery and Ceramics. Sculpting. Digital Art. Beadwork. Besides encouraging creativity, engagement with art has been found to improve mental health, trauma symptoms, pain management and social cohesion, according to the American Psychological Association.

This summer, consider picking up a pen, paintbrush or stylus. Those interested can also attend events focused on art.

The 60th annual Central Pennsylvania Festival of the Arts is being held from July 8 to July 12 in State College. Arts Fest, which is held downtown every July, is a colorful State College tradition featuring artists and curious creatives.

Geoguessing

If you’re often told you’re good with directions, why not put that to the test?

Online geoguessing games confront you with a random, unlabeled location and ask you to find your place on the map based on context clues.

Understanding places can be one way of understanding people, and geoguessing is a way to add to your cultural capital outside of an academic context.

Cinema

Summer is the season of movie theaters, popcorn and the disorienting feeling of cinema lights coming back on as credits roll. If you’re not already a regular movie watcher, summer is the perfect time to see what’s playing in your local movie theater.

New releases include “Obsession,” “Backrooms,” “Michael” and “Masters of The Universe.” “Supergirl” will also arrive in theaters this week.

Indoor rock climbing

Rock climbing has been found to improve grip strength and physical fitness. Besides being a form of exercise that can make you more adept at others, indoor rock climbing also shields you from the sun. This is an option for those who prefer more active hobbies.

If you do start climbing, make sure you stay safe. Falls can sometimes be dangerous, so wear the appropriate gear and practice in supervised environments.

Music

Have you ever thought it would be cool to play piano, accordion or electric guitar? Regardless of what instrument catches your fancy, musical training can improve cognitive functioning or simply add joy to your summer.

If you already have an instrument on hand, brush off the dust, find a tutorial on YouTube and get started.

Pick up a language

Besides being a fun, new thing to try, picking up another language can benefit your social life, improve your economic opportunities and ward off cognitive decline, according to the National Library of Medicine.

This summer, consider downloading a language app, signing up for a class or binging a Netflix series in another language. As a bonus, there are a variety of language-learning clubs at Penn State that await your proficient return.

Baking

If you enjoy baked goods and sweet treats, why not learn how to make them yourself? Recipes are abundant online but, if you prefer more tactile resources, pick up a cookbook or two.

At the very least, your tastebuds and your loved ones might thank you.

Photography

As established, summer can be a time to visit new places and try new things. If you’re traveling this summer, or if you’re simply making new memories, getting into photography (iPhone or otherwise) can help you immortalize the moments you’d like to remember.

Water Sports

If there’s a safe and supervised body of water near you, getting into aquatic activities can cool you off and work out your heart. Consider adding water sports like swimming, water polo or water volleyball to your repertoire if you’re looking to stay active and cool this summer.

title: State Theatre events from June 29 to July 5
author: Jackson Malenke | The Daily Collegian
description:
State Theatre is hosting movies ranging from musicals, dramas, thrillers and comedies this week.

Here’s what each day has to offer.

Wednesday, July 1

“Wicked”

State Theatre is continuing its partnership with Schlow Library by frequently bringing children’s movies to the screen on Wednesdays.

This week, the theatre is showing “Wicked,” a 2024 movie adaptation of the award-winning musical of the same name. The movie follows the Wicked Witch of the West during the events leading up to “The Wizard of Oz.”

The event is free with registration on the website and begins at noon.

Thursday, July 2

“Stand By Me”

“Stand By Me” is being shown in the Friedman Auditorium at 7:30 p.m.

The 1986 Rob Reiner film follows a group of friends in search of a missing teenager. This search turns into a two-day journey of self discovery. It’s based on Stephen King’s 1982 novel “The Body.”

Friday, July 3

“Jaws”

This 1975 classic by Steven Spielberg is returning to the State Theatre at 8 p.m.

The movie follows the community of Amity while a great white shark lurks in its waters.

Sunday, July 5

“The Sandlot”

At 2:30 p.m. on Sunday, State Theatre is showing the heartwarming comedy, “The Sandlot.”

The film follows Scotty Smalls, who just moved into town and is struggling to make friends, until he is recruited to play baseball on the sandlot field.

Tickets are available online or at the door for each showing. Adult tickets are $12.68 and student tickets are $10.58.

title: SEE IT:  Gavin McKenna throws first pitch at Toronto Blue Jays game
author: Andrew Deal | The Daily Collegian
description:
Gavin McKenna is already getting acclimated in Toronto.

The first overall pick tossed the first pitch at the Toronto Blue Jays' game against the Texas Rangers on Saturday.

McKenna was drafted with the No. 1 overall pick in the draft by the Toronto Maple Leafs after spending his freshman season with Penn State.

The forward put up 51 points across 35 games with the Nittany Lions, totalling 36 assists. He set the freshman program record for points in a season, while also recording the program record for assists in a season and points in a game, with eight against Ohio State.

The Whitehorse, Yukon, native is expected to join the Maple Leafs for the 2026-27 season, instantly becoming one of the faces of the historic franchise.

title: Penn State commit Quinn McKenzie selected by New Jersey Devils in 2026 NHL Draft
author: Elliott Inscho | Sports Editor
description:
An eighth player with Penn State ties has been drafted.

Forward Quinn McKenzie was selected with the No. 222 overall pick in the 2026 NHL Draft by the New Jersey Devils on Saturday.

McKenzie is an 18-year-old Maryland native who spent time with Pittsburgh Penguins Elite early in his hockey career.

This past season, he played with the OHL’s Soo Greyhounds, posting 51 points in 61 games.

He’s projected to join the Nittany Lions ahead of the 2026-27 season.

MORE HOCKEY COVERAGE

title: Penn State commit Alexander Karmanov selected by San Jose Sharks in 2026 NHL Draft
author: Andrew Deal | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State's tallest commit has been selected in the NHL draft.

Defenseman commit Alexander Karmanov was drafted by the San Jose Sharks with the 201st pick in the seventh round of the draft.

The 7-foot-1 defenseman becomes the tallest player ever drafted in the NHL. He also becomes the first player from Moldova to be selected in the NHL draft.

Karmanov was a staggering defender for the North Bay Battalion of the OHL, but he played just 20 games, recording two points and a plus-6.

The defenseman will return to North Bay for the 2026-27 season before joining Penn State for the 2027-28 season.

MORE HOCKEY COVERAGE

title: Penn State commit Caden Harvey selected by Edmonton Oilers in 2026 NHL Draft
author: Elliott Inscho | Sports Editor
description:
Yet another Penn State commit heard his name called in the 2026 NHL Draft.

Forward Caden Harvey was selected by the Edmonton Oilers in the sixth round on Saturday with the No. 180 overall pick.

Harvey played with the OHL’s Windsor Spitfires last season, scoring eleven goals and recording 23 assists. He’s a Beaver, Pennsylvania, native that spent time with Pittsburgh Vengeance and Pittsburgh Penguins Elite before moving to the OHL.

Harvey is projected to join Guy Gadowsky’s Nittany Lions next season.

title: Penn State commit Ryder Fetterolf selected by Carolina Hurricanes in 2026 NHL Draft
author: The Daily Collegian
description:
Another member of Guy Gadowsky’s future squad is joining an NHL organization.

Goaltender Ryder Fetterolf, a Pennsylvania native, has joined four other players with Penn State ties in this year's NHL draft, being selected in the fourth round of the 2026 NHL Draft to the Carolina Hurricanes.

The netminder is coming off a strong season with the OHL’s Ottawa 67's. His six shutouts on the season set a new OHL rookie single-season record.

Along with the six clean sheets, he had a save percentage of .923 and a goals-against average of 2.07. He took home some hardware at the end of the season, including OHL and CHL Goaltender of the Year, the Dinty Moore Trophy and the Dave Pinkey Trophy.

The lefty goalie has a smaller presence in net, only standing at 6-foot. While he has a height disadvantage, his quick style of play, puck tracking abilities and flexibility has allowed him to remain a top goaltender in the Juniors.

Elite Prospects ranked Fetterolf No. 180 in the class, with Smaht Scouting ranked him No. 84.

Among North American goaltenders, NHL Central Scouting ranked him No. 11. The instinctive player specializes in coming up with two to three saves in a single play and has been recognized by scouts for his resilience.

Before joining the professional level, the Pittsburgh powerhouse will remain relatively local as he'll head to Happy Valley.

MORE HOCKEY COVERAGE

title: Penn State commit Pierce Mbuyi selected by Pittsburgh Penguins in 2026 NHL Draft
author: Myles Hannak | The Daily Collegian
description:
Another Nittany Lion commit has been drafted.

Left winger Pierce Mbuyi was selected in the third round of the 2026 NHL Draft by the Pittsburgh Penguins, joining fellow commits Jaxon Cover and Yegor Shilov as future blue and white freshmen to hear their names called so far in Buffalo, New York.

Mbuyi was ranked as the No. 56 North American skater in this year’s draft class by NHL.com, following a strong youth career. The Mississauga, Ontario, native spent the last two seasons with the Owen Sound Attack in the OHL, where he earned Rookie of the Year honors in his debut campaign.

Last year, he posted 75 points, with 32 goals over his 68 total games in the OHL, and also appeared for the Canadian U18 squad in the Hlinka Gretzky Cup. With his country, Mbuyi logged one point over four games and helped secure bronze.

At 5-foot-11 and 163 pounds, Mbuyi’s frame may appear limited, yet at the NHL Draft Combine earlier this month, he tested as one of the most physically strong players, posting top-five outputs in the isokinetic squat and bench press power.

The lefty uses that strength to complement a polished offensive game that can put the puck behind the goaltender by any means, as he usually slots alongside a playmaking piece.

Mbuyi is projected to be join the Nittany Lions ahead of the 2027-28 season, as he'll be headed back to Owen Sound for another OHL season.

MORE HOCKEY COVERAGE

title: Penn State commit Egor Shilov selected by Colorado Avalanche in 2026 NHL Draft
author: Andrew Mercer | The Daily Collegian
description:
Guy Gadowsky’s forward-to-be is a second-round draft pick.

Egor Shilov was selected with the No. 43 overall pick by the Colorado Avalanche in the 2026 NHL Draft. The Tyumen, Russia, native was the third player with ties to Penn State to be drafted.

The 18-year-old center recently took home the QMJHL Offensive Rookie of the Year award with the Victoriaville Tigres. He racked up 82 points in 63 games in his first season up north.

Before the draft, Shilov was ranked a late first round to early second round pick by various outlets including Elite Prospects, TSN, The Hockey News and more.

Standing at 6-foot-1, Shilov is seen as a premier dangler and playmaker, and is able to slow the pace of the game to allow for more technical moves to shine.

Shilov flipped his commitment from Boston University to Penn State on May 14, but won’t be joining the Nittany Lions until the 2027-28 season.

In the meantime, he’s set to play one more season in Victoriaville to further his development in juniors before making the jump to college hockey.

MORE HOCKEY COVERAGE

title: Penn State commit Jaxon Cover selected by Ottawa Senators in 2026 NHL Draft
author: Myles Hannak | The Daily Collegian
description:
A future Nittany Lion is now a professional prospect.

After former Penn State winger Gavin McKenna went No. 1 overall to the Toronto Maple Leafs, Jaxon Cover marked the second player with Penn State ties off the board.

The winger was selected by the Ottawa Senators with the final pick in the first round after a strong OHL season boosted his draft stock.

As the No. 45 North American Skater in NHL.com’s midseason rankings, he improved that standing by 16 spots by the final standings. Cover spent only one full season in the OHL with the London Knights. He posted 52 points over 67 games last season, earning First All-Rookie Team honors.

The 6-foot-2, 183-lb skater is tabbed as a developing prospect, with a creative offensive game that can still be molded further.

Before the draft, Cover was profiled by The Athletic for his story about growing up in the Cayman Islands and developing his hockey skills in a nation with little background in the sport, as he himself didn’t play ice hockey until he was 13.

Since then, Cover has been one of the fastest risers in this draft, while in his first year of eligibility. The lefty will join Happy Valley next season as a member of the Senators’ organization to continue his development before going on to the pros.

MORE HOCKEY COVERAGE

title: THON 2027 dates announced
author: Michelle Diamond | News Editor
description:
THON, Penn State’s annual 46-hour dance marathon “for the kids,” will take place Feb. 19 through 21, 2027.

Every spring semester, Penn State hosts the largest student-run philanthropy in the world for the fight against childhood cancer. Students gather in the Bryce Jordan Center to stand for a cure to raise money for Four Diamonds.

During THON 2026, Penn State raised $18,841,726.53, setting a new record. With roughly eight months until the weekend arrives, students have already begun preparing to surpass this total.

MORE NEWS COVERAGE

title: Gavin McKenna selected No. 1 overall by Toronto Maple Leafs in 2026 NHL Draft
author: Myles Hannak | The Daily Collegian
description:
Gavin McKenna is headed back to the Great White North, trading one blue and white for another.

Penn State forward Gavin McKenna was selected with the No. 1 overall pick in the 2026 NHL Draft by the Toronto Maple Leafs on Friday night, marking a generational moment in the Nittany Lions’ history.

After a one-year stint in Happy Valley, where McKenna had a freshman year of unprecedented magnitude both on and off the ice, his collegiate chapter is now presumably closed. He set a new Penn State freshman record with 51 points in 2026, and was named the 2026 Big Ten Freshman of the Year and an all-conference second team skater.

His impact wasn’t just on the stat sheet. In only one season, McKenna became a cultural phenomenon never seen before on the ice in Happy Valley. From brand deals and TV coverage, he put eyes on the program like never before as Division I hockey’s most anticipated prospect in recent memory.

McKenna accomplished most of this before turning 18, which he did mid-season, dispelling any doubts that came with that youth as he arrived in Happy Valley.

His year came to a peak in the first-ever hockey game at Beaver Stadium, and with the national eye on Penn State and 74,000 fans in attendance, McKenna scored a goal and provided a pair of assists in an overtime thriller against Michigan State.

That on-ice moment was one of many that will be remembered in Penn State history, from his first NCAA goal, a game-winner, to his program and conference-record-setting eight-point game against Ohio State.

“For what Penn State has done for me, I can’t thank this team and the entire community enough,” McKenna said after Minnesota-Duluth defeated Penn State in the postseason. “I’m a Penn Stater for life.”

From the wooden stick that coach Guy Gadowsky kept at pressers, reading “McKenna Magic” during his Hobey Baker campaign, to his postgame Taco Bell orders, or his work on the ping-pong table in the locker room, McKenna’s presence brought a unique atmosphere to Penn State’s 2026 season, one never seen before.

“I hope I left my legacy there,” McKenna said at the pre-draft scouting combine, “Penn State was a fun ride, to get to experience some crazy things, like the outdoor game, and college in general.”

It wasn’t just at the collegiate level where McKenna showcased his dominance this year.

At the 2026 IIHF World Junior Championships, he posted the second-highest point total in the competition with 14 points for a U20 Canada team that earned bronze.

“Playing with Gavin is pretty special, what he does with the puck, the way he carries himself out there – it's pretty incredible,” Keaton Verhoeff, McKenna’s teammate with Canada and fellow draft prospect, said at the combine. “Even sitting there and watching him, you kinda find yourself in awe.”

With his selection, McKenna is now the first-ever Nittany Lion to be taken in the first round of the NHL Draft after their collegiate tenure. The new highest-ever selection for the program beat out Charlie Cerrato’s second-round selection to the Carolina Hurricanes in 2025, as well as defenseman Jackson Smith, who was drafted in the first round of last season's draft as well, but hadn’t yet suited up for Penn State.

For the university, McKenna joins football alumni Ki-Jana Carter and Courtney Brown as the only athletes from Penn State to be selected in the top spot of their sport’s draft. The blue and white are now also the just third university to send a No. 1 selection in the NHL Draft and NFL Draft, joining Michigan and Michigan State.

While this is uncharted territory for Penn State, McKenna was already a No. 1 overall draft selection when taken by the Medicine Hat Tigers in the 2022 WHL Draft.

McKenna had a remarkable junior career and ended with a bang, surpassing those lofty expectations. In 2025, he set a modern-era Canadian Hockey League record for point streak and finished with a points-per-game figure that was the third best posted by a 17-year-old in WHL history.

For the CHL, it’s the second straight season that one of its former skaters has been selected No. 1 overall in the NHL Draft, following the New York Islanders’ selection of 2026 Calder Memorial Trophy winner Matthew Schaefer.

Although for McKenna, his position as No. 1 was forecast for years and had even been included in mocks after last year’s draft. Throughout all that’s happened since, he hasn’t missed a beat or seen that prediction slip. Now, the next step of his hockey journey is underway.

The prodigy will arrive in Toronto, a city that desperately needs him. Before the first puck drop of 2026, the Maple Leafs were expected to regress after the departure of Mitch Marner via a sign-and-trade with the Las Vegas Golden Knights, but things got much worse than expected.

With key offensive pieces Austin Matthews and William Nylander missing time due to injuries, Toronto finished 32-36-14, eighth in the Atlantic Division.

“Obviously, it would be an honor, as a kid, that’s what you dream of,” McKenna said at the scouting combine when asked about the chance to go to Toronto. “If that’s the case, I’d be pretty pumped, obviously, being a Canadian kid going to a Canadian market will be pretty special.”

Despite their woes, the Maple Leafs entered the draft lottery with only the fifth-best odds of landing the top pick, but the ping-pong balls bounced their way.

“It’s pretty crazy they got the first pick,” McKenna said at the combine, “They’re a team that’s probably going to be fighting for the playoffs next year. I’d be fortunate to go there.”

McKenna will take everything he learned in Happy Valley with him to Toronto, leaving a meaningful mark on program history.

title: Lexi’s Law | Summer fashion has officially gotten out of control
author: Lexi Cannan | The Daily Collegian
description:
Ever wish you had a stylish big sister? The one whose closet you constantly steal from and whose opinion somehow ends up being right every time?

Welcome to Lexi’s Law, your guide to fashion, beauty, trends and pop culture. Every week, I’ll tell you what’s hot, what’s not and occasionally save you from a fashion mistake you’ll regret later.

Beach vacations create this strange pressure to completely reinvent yourself. Everyone wants to become “that girl” for the summer: perfectly tan, perfectly dressed, perfectly tidy despite the humidity. That’s where summer fashion starts to lose the plot.

The rest of us who know it isn’t a reality to pull that off are fighting for our lives with frizzy hair and claw clips hanging on by a thread.

Getting dressed used to be the easy part, at least for me, but it now seems like every outfit needs to be worthy of a vacation photo dump. So instead of one outfit being good enough, my hamper is now overflowing on the daily because the beach trip, the dinner reservation and the designated Instagram photoshoot time somehow all need separate outfits.

Is it really worth ripping apart your closet for a stranger to take one glance at you and maybe, maybe spend their precious time for maybe, maybe two seconds to think about how you look? And an even better question, is it worth the discomfort?

I’ll tell you what: Clothes that require adjusting every four seconds are not worth it, especially those teeny, tiny tops. If you cannot physically laugh, sit down, or eat mozzarella sticks comfortably, the outfit has failed. It doesn’t matter how it looks.

Clearly, I hate that summer fashion has become way too complicated. I yearn for a day when summer trends are about wearing clothes that actually make you look and feel great. The girls with the best style usually aren’t chasing every trend, watching the number in their bank account decline, because they're too busy knowing what looks good on them.

But from my personal observations — as someone who maybe, maybe does spend time looking at what other people are wearing — I’ve got a few tips on making summer fashion less time-consuming.

For starters, oversized button-downs are basically the MVP. They hide sunburns and instantly make any outfit look better, but they’re still effortless.

Gold hoops, simple tanks and matching sets will always look good, and they’re usually staples in girls’ closets, meaning you have a thousand to quickly grab off the hanger. And matching sets make it look like you have your life together. (Even if your phone is on 2% and you forgot sunscreen again. Totally not speaking from personal experience.)

A sundress for dinner, a cute bikini with jewelry and an oversized tote bag that carries your entire life are the best ingredients for a successful beach trip. While those teeny, tiny tops are trendy, simple white tanks deserve more respect than they’re given for the mere fact that they won’t suck the life and joy out of you.

A lightweight sweater for when the beach gets cold at night is another nonnegotiable. Every girl brings one, then forgets she brought one and ends up stealing someone else’s. But if you’re really all that concerned with your outfit looking good, bring your own. At least that way you’ll still be in your own style, which will, in turn, make you more comfortable.

I don’t want to tell you what to do. Really, fashion is individualistic. That’s what makes it fun, and that’s what has kept it a creative field since the moment we developed the concept. But some things aren’t meant to coexist — like denim and sand. There is literally no need to wear jeans to the beach.

Here’s another one: If your outfit requires fashion tape, two friends pulling a zipper simultaneously and a complete loss of circulation, it’s not “giving effortless” the way you think it is.

The same goes for shoes that give blisters before you can even leave the house. Why wear the shoes if you’ll be carrying them down the boardwalk later in the night? Your hands are pretty much the only body part that shouldn’t be wearing your clothes.

While we’re here, leather needs to take the summer off. Nobody is looking at someone sweating through leather pants in July and thinking, “Wow, she looks comfortable.”

Yes, I hold the simultaneous opinion that summer fashion should be what makes you comfortable while still subscribing to at least some standard. The truth is that confidence will always look better than whatever random microtrend TikTok discovered and pushed out to you three days ago. Wear what makes you feel good, wear what makes sense and stop letting the internet convince you that you need a completely new personality every summer. Instead, let me!

I’m kidding. Most of what I’m saying will probably be out by Labor Day, so soak in those summer rays, and forget about your (and my) worries.

The clothes you wear on your body don’t matter half as much as the confidence you wear on your shoulders. Consider that Lexi’s Law.

title: 40 year anniversary special | Reflecting on the legacy of Penn State’s 1986 national championship team
author: Lexie Linderman | Football Editor
description:
The last time Penn State won a national championship, I was still 19 years away from being born, and my father was just 6-years-old. It has been four decades since the Nittany Lions were at the top of the mountain.

So much has happened for Penn State since — both good and bad — that 1986 feels like a distant memory for fans (who were alive, at least). But I think it’s important to remember just how special this team was.

That’s why I wanted us to do a series on this team for its 40 year anniversary. No, I wasn’t there. But neither was any current student or recent graduate at Penn State, so why not try to enlighten them a little bit about the last time the Nittany Lions won the national championship?

The 1986 team was the epitome of Penn State football. It had an elite defense with one of the best linebackers in the country, coupled with an offense that relied heavily on the rushing attack. Sound familiar?

That linebacker was Shane Conlan, who remains one of the greatest linebackers to ever play for the Nittany Lions. He’s part of the reason Penn State is regarded as “Linebacker U,” and it most likely doesn’t win the 1986 national championship without him or his two interceptions in the Fiesta Bowl.

Penn State is very lucky Tom Bradley convinced Joe Paterno to offer Conlan out of high school.

Conlan is also the only player from the national-championship winning squad to be inducted into the NFF College Football Hall of Fame, and he went on to have easily the best NFL career among the group.

Speaking of the NFL, the Nittany Lions had a record 13 players selected in the 1987 NFL Draft. That’s a record that I personally can’t see ever being broken, although it was a 12-round draft. The closest Penn State has ever gotten to that number since was 10 picks in 1996.

The big statement from this team was of course the upset over Miami to win the Fiesta Bowl and the national championship — there’s no denying that. Forcing seven turnovers, five of which were interceptions thrown by Heisman Trophy winner Vinny Testaverde, is absurd when facing the No. 1 team in the country.

That game is obviously a huge part of the 1986 team’s legacy, and it’s what most people think of when this season is brought up. It’s hard to forget a game-sealing interception with 18 seconds left.

But I think Penn State’s close win over Notre Dame in the regular season is what prepared it for the Fiesta Bowl. The Nittany Lions came back to defeat one of their biggest rivals, and it was the closest game they had up until playing Miami.

Every good team can dominate, but every great team is resilient, and that’s what Penn State showed against the Fighting Irish and the Hurricanes.

Above all, the Nittany Lions’ victory in 1986 cemented them as a powerhouse in college football. It gave them two titles in five years, and they did it by winning one of the greatest games ever.

It’s a team that every Penn State fan should look back on once in a while with a lot of pride, and it’s a season that every Penn State fan hopes can be replicated soon.

title: Exploring the WNBA careers of Penn State women’s basketball coaches
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Correction: A previous version of this article stated the four coaches were former Lady Lions. However, Octavia Blue, an assistant coach, is not a former Lady Lion. The Daily Collegian apologizes for this error.

Not only does Penn State have several alumni on its coaching staff this season, it has several former professional athletes coaching the next generation.

Between the four former stars, the coaches have 26 years of WNBA experience and three league titles. Outside of the WNBA, they’ve spent many years playing internationally, as well.

Here’s a look at each of the four Lady Lions coaches and their careers in the WNBA.

Tanisha Wright: 2005-2019

Penn State’s seventh head coach in program history kicked off her professional career in 2005 when she was drafted 12th overall to the Seattle Storm in the first round.

Tanisha Wright spent a majority of her career with the Storm, playing in 330 games over 10 years. In Seattle, Wright made over 200 career starts and earned several awards, including Sixth Player of the Year in 2008, Most Improved Player in 2009 and Defensive Player of the Year in 2013.

The West Mifflin, Pennsylvania, native averaged double-digit scoring during three seasons with the Storm. In 2009, she recorded 12.2 points, 3.5 rebounds, 3.5 assists and 1.5 steals per game.

The highlight of Wright’s stint in Seattle came in 2010, when the Storm won the WNBA Championship, sweeping the Atlanta Dream in three games.

Following the 2014 season, Wright was signed by the New York Liberty in free agency. She spent two years with the Liberty before taking the 2017 season off, playing for the Minnesota Lynx in 2018 and returning to the Liberty for her final season in 2019.

Kelly Mazzante: 2004-2011

Despite leading the Lady Lions to four straight NCAA Tournament appearances as Penn State’s all-time leading scorer, Kelly Mazzante had a short and less-than-ideal WNBA career.

Mazzante was drafted to the Charlotte Sting with the 18th pick in the second round where she spent the next three years. The Montoursville, Pennsylvania, native started just two games with 95 appearances, averaging 4.7 points per game.

After winning Most Improved Player of the Year in 2006, averaging 8.9 points per game, Mazzante went fifth overall in the 2007 dispersal draft to the Phoenix Mercury.

In Phoenix, Mazzante made seven starts across three seasons, averaging more playing time than in Charlotte. She recorded 5.4, 5.8 and 3.4 points from 2007 to 2009, respectively, winning WNBA titles in her first and last seasons with the Mercury.

Mazzante missed the 2010 season while rehabbing her ACL, but finished her career with the Atlanta Dream, where she played in just six games.

Angie Potthoff: 1999-2000

Angie Potthoff had a very short professional career in the WNBA, playing just two seasons in Year 3 and Year 4 of the league.

Penn State’s new chief of staff was drafted 49th overall to the Minnesota Lynx in the fourth round, following two years in the American Basketball League.

Potthoff made 25 starts in her first season, recording four points and 2.7 rebounds per game. However, she saw action in just four games her second season, before ending her professional career.

Octavia Blue: 1998-2004

While Octavia Blue played in the WNBA over a span of seven years, she truly only saw three seasons of action in the league.

Blue was drafted 15th overall to the Los Angeles Sparks in the second season of the WNBA, where she started three games and had 30 appearances. The Fort Lauderdale, Florida, native recorded just 2.4 points and 1.2 rebounds per game.

The following season, Blue was waived by the Sparks, taken in the expansion draft by the Minnesota Lynx and waived again. She wasn’t on a WNBA roster until 2003, when she was picked up by the Houston Comets.

Blue played 29 games across two seasons for the Comets, making a bigger impact in Year 2 with 3.4 points in 11.9 minutes per game.

title: CATA to launch new transport options after Penn State Transportation ceases Mount Nittany Health shuttle services
author: Anisa Daniel-Oniko | The Daily Collegian
description:
Correction: A previous version of this article stated CATA was ceasing a shuttle service to Mount Nittany Medical Center on Park Avenue and Mount Nittany Health Toftrees. Penn State Transportation is ceasing the service; CATA in collaboration with Penn State is now launching different transport options.

Penn State Transportation will cease operating a shuttle service to Mount Nittany Medical Center on Park Avenue and Mount Nittany Health Toftrees on Tuesday, according to a CATA press release.

CATA has coordinated with Penn State to create “a demand-response service for those who are not within a CATAGO! service zone to Mount Nittany Medical Center,” according to the press release.

“CATA is pleased to offer this new option for getting to and from the hospital because it will both prevent a gap in transit service that would otherwise occur on July 1, and it guarantees access to Mount Nittany Medical Center for every person in CATA's service area,” CATA CEO Todd Horsley said in a press release.

The service, which requires riders within the designated service area to schedule trips in advance, will operate from 7:00 a.m. to 8:00 p.m. daily. The fare of $2.50 per one-way trip is equal to a regular CATA fare.

Reservations will be made available up to two weeks before travel until the evening prior to the trip.

CATAGO! will also make trips to Mount Nittany Health Toftrees, available for riders within the Center Area West CATAGO! service zone. Given the H Route access to this facility will be discontinued on August 17, this service will provide an alternative transport.

Transfers between CATABUS and CATAGO! services will still be available at no additional cost. They are only available for up to an hour for the continuation of one-way trips.

For more information and service maps, visit its site.

title: Off the record | Just-average junior
author: Miss Behave I The Daily Collegian
description:
Editor’s Note: Off the record is a weekly column written by a fourth-year Collegian staffer under an alias. It aims to promote a space for honest conversations about love, sex and relationships. Those interested in sharing their experiences or submitting a topic can do so through this anonymous form.

Dear just-average junior,

Right off the bat, the thing that stands out to me from your submission is the phrase “college experience.”

The college experience presented on television and social media isn’t necessarily real, and it’s tainting your real-life experience. You’re creating a picture in your head of what college should look like, and in order for you to truly be happy, you have to be in touch with reality, with what’s in front of you.

Creating unrealistic expectations about your time at Penn State is only making your day-to-day seem boring or not as cool as your peers because you’re convinced they’re boring.

Not every day is filled with Instagram-worthy photoshoots. Sometimes, you wake up at noon and watch reality TV until you force yourself to go outside and reconnect with nature. Other days are filled with late nights in the library doing the project you swore you could complete in a night. (News flash, you can’t.)

The minute you stop comparing yourself is the minute you’ll start feeling better.

The social aspect of college is crucial, so I understand why it feels as if you don’t have true, real friends. Remember: Those friend groups of 50 girls aren’t real best friends and it's true what they say: quality over quantity.

Oftentimes, people act as though the only thing that matters is the tailgates you're invited to, or what social organizations you're in. The future you’re working toward matters more than how much you partied.

Also, not everyone is going to be your friend, and it is entirely fine if they aren’t. If someone doesn’t want to be your friend, don’t force it and instead channel your energy elsewhere.

Truthfully, I don’t think class is where you should be looking for friends with whom you do social activities. Most people have their core group already established, but this doesn’t mean there’s no potential for new friends.

Clubs are going to be the key to your success. While it’s important to fill your resume with professional opportunities, not every club you join needs to be a resume-booster. If you like sewing, join a sewing club, if you like to play volleyball, join an intramural league. Exploring your hobbies is going to lead you to people who feel the same. It takes a cognizant effort to cultivate a group of friends who feel authentic, and it’s not going to happen overnight.

Worst case scenario, you go to one meeting and don’t like it. Nothing is so finite that you're tied to it for the rest of the year. Just don’t go again! It’s that simple.

While the professional clubs seem great from the outside, there’s no guarantee you actually like the people in them. I would hate for you to think that entrance to these clubs automatically means there are friends to be made, or you’ll find your people, because that’s what they advertise.

Forcing yourself out of your comfort zone is uncomfortable, and for someone who acknowledges they're on the quieter side, it’s more important. It’s not going to come as easily as someone outgoing, which means you’re going to work at it twice as hard.

Many people have luck on trips or smaller groups with close proximity, and reoccurring meetings. Do your best to find a familiar face.

Those awkward moments and late nights where you’re stuck thinking “why’d I say that” will pay off, and you’ll find the people you’ve wished for.

I certainly don’t think your experience in college is going to be representative of the rest of your life. If it feels like that, I’m sorry. You deserve a group of friends who make you welcome and loved. Just because it doesn’t feel like that now doesn’t mean it’ll never happen; you have the rest of your life to create those relationships, and when you find them, they’ll feel that much sweeter.

As you go into your senior year, be patient and know you’re not the only ones with these feelings. You’ll wake up in 10, 15, 20 years and none of this will matter.

You’ll find the people who make you laugh until you can’t breathe, who you can tell your deepest secrets to, who will remind you that the wait was worth it.

I promise.

Until next time,

Miss Behave.

title: Penn State hockey draft profile | Gavin McKenna’s playmaking, superior vision key to NHL transition
author: Andrew Mercer | The Daily Collegian
description:
Gavin McKenna reminded the world why he’s the projected No. 1 overall pick in the 2026 NHL Entry Draft in the late February series against Ohio State. On that Friday, McKenna showed off his playmaking skills by racking up seven assists and a goal on the night, a Penn State single-game record.

The following day, when the game went to overtime, McKenna took over. He executed a stunning toe-drag move to get past the defenseman before finding the back of the net on a tight-angle wrist shot to send the Buckeyes packing.

The young sensation from Whitehorse, Yukon, came to Penn State with the expectations of bringing the Nittany Lions back to the Frozen Four and potentially winning a national championship. Although he started the season with three points in two games against Arizona State, his effectiveness often fluctuated, leaving many in doubt of his ability to transfer his skills to a more physical game.

While the season didn’t pan out in the way that Penn State wanted it to, McKenna still made his mark on the program. He was named a top-10 finalist for the Hobey Baker Award for the nation’s top player, was named the Big Ten Scoring Champion with 38 points in 24 conference games and the program’s first Big Ten Freshman of the Year.

McKenna built momentum all the way through his first, and potentially only, collegiate season. He enters the NHL draft with more experience against older, larger players, and is expected to make an immediate impact as a generational talent.

“My goal is to play in the NHL, and if my team wants me to sign, then that’s my goal, whether that happens next year or not,” McKenna said.

Bio & metrics

Hometown: Whitehorse, Yukon

DOB: 12/20/2007

Height: 5-foot-11

Shot: Left

NCAA statistics

Games played: 35

Goals: 15

Assists: 36

Points: 51

Shot percentage: .099

+/-: -7

Penalty mins: 36

Awards

IIHF World U18 Championship gold medalist (2024)

Hobey Baker Award Top-10 Finalist

AHCA West Second Team All-American

Big Ten Freshman of the Year

Big Ten Scoring Champion

All-Big Ten Second Team

All-Big Ten Freshman Team

CHL David Branch Player of the Year (2024-25)

WHL Four Broncos Memorial Trophy (2024-25)

CHL Rookie of the Year (2023-24)

Ed Chynoweth Cup (WHL) Champion (2024-25)

Projections

Projected round drafted: No. 1 overall pick

Player comparison: Patrick Kane

Scouting report

The hockey world knew what McKenna would bring to college and the NHL from his time with the Medicine Hat Tigers of the WHL. He’s a tactical playmaker that focuses on controlling the puck with superior stickhandling skills and sharp edgework. He’s used it effectively throughout his career to open doors for a well-timed shot or perfectly-placed pass into space.

The “Wizard of Whitehorse” systematically breaks down defenses one layer at a time, but not by himself. Every pass that he makes takes one defender out of the equation until he’s able to maneuver himself into position to test the goaltender.

Speaking of his shot — it’s lethal. He has a deceivingly fast release, and when the shot comes, he pulls it closer to his body to get the goalie moving laterally before pinpointing the corners of the net for the best chance to score.

McKenna displays great vision in finding open players, even when he’s in a prime scoring position himself. There have been times when he’s received a pass in the middle of the circle, but finds an open teammate streaking deeper in the slot in front of the crease. McKenna’s game isn’t just about scoring goals, it’s creating opportunities and spreading the wealth.

He sees the game like a chess match, always thinking two steps ahead while working himself into position to be most effective. He’s patient and tends to slow down the pace of play while looking for options, but isn’t afraid to kickstart the engines to catch teams off guard.

One area of his game that may be seen as lacking is his physicality and size. When bigger defenders lay the body on him, McKenna is usually knocked out of the play and turns the puck over. He supplements this by making use of his edgework and stickhandling, but that can only get him so far, especially when contact is unavoidable.

However, this story has happened time and time again, notably with Jack Hughes, an undersized forward who had an adjustment period early in his career due to his lack of size compared to the league-average defender. That being said, Hughes is now among the NHL’s elite, so who's to say that McKenna can’t reach those heights.

Best team fit: Vancouver Canucks

Toronto’s No. 1 overall pick aside, McKenna’s best team fit is the Vancouver Canucks. When looking at the Canucks’ roster, their top wingers being Jake DeBrusk and Brock Boeser, the team doesn’t have a standout franchise player that's producing at a point per game level.

Vancouver’s top center, Elias Pettersson, would be a solid fit as McKenna’s debut linemate and that connection could be the key to unlocking the first line’s skill ceiling. With McKenna’s vision and creativity, combined with Pettersson being one of the best shooters in the NHL, Vancouver would be able to build the team around those two players.

However, it’s worth mentioning that the Canucks have the No. 3 overall pick in the draft, and are going to likely miss out on the former Nittany Lion. A more probable pick for Vancouver would be Caleb Malhotra, a center who’s committed to play for Boston University, who would fill the second line center role behind Petterson or make the switch to the wing.

title: Fein-ding myself | Everything I’ve always been
author: Rebecca Fein | The Daily Collegian
description:
As a child, I always yearned to be older.

When I was in elementary school, I dreamed about middle school, having my own locker and nine different teachers a day instead of being confined to a single classroom. When I was in middle school, I couldn’t wait until the day I went to high school. The last stage before I was free from the reigns of mom and dad, finally letting my independence shine.

Now, I’ve made it. I'm doing what I always said I would do, at the place I always said I would do it, but I can’t help but turn around to look at all the people I have left behind, the people who helped me get here.

I always thought of college as a fresh start, a place to reinvent the old version of yourself into the person you’ve always wanted to be. Don’t get me wrong, college is still a place to start over, but when I got to Penn State and looked around, I realized that these “new” people around me already had 18 years of love, 18 years of loss, 18 years of experience.

As badly as I may have wanted to forget certain memories and times in my life, it’s impossible. If you do manage to forget, a piece of everyone you’ve ever loved is still inside of you somewhere. Everything you do is a direct result of what has come before you.

My childhood best friend I lost touch with, my coworkers from my very first job and even those who have passed on have all instilled a lesson in me, even when my memory lapses.

Upon realizing that my old self will always be a part of the new one, I began to embrace the people who were in front of me. I realized letting strangers in allows for growth and new opportunities, and there’s actually some strange, unexpected kind of joy in telling fresh faces worn stories.

When I came to college, my two best friends were people I had grown up with my whole life: my mom and dad. Not that I didn’t love them to bits, but it was evident I needed new relationships to educate me on pieces of life I have not yet lived.

As a result, I let everyone in. Sorority sisters, strangers from class, boyfriends, people in clubs I joined and especially those that I lived with. Not every relationship will last forever, but that doesn't mean it wasn’t important in leaving a mark on my life.

People say college is about “becoming yourself,” but I believe it is actually about realizing you are already yourself, from the places you’ve been, people you’ve met and experiences you’ve had.

Identity is shared. It’s hard to know who you are when you are living in a stage of life that is constantly changing, but there will always be one constant: You are a million memories, stories, moments and lessons of those you let in.

I’m not saying you have to be friends with absolutely everyone — sometimes, it’s the temporary connections that leave the most permanent mark — but life is more about embracing the unknown and pushing beyond the walls of your comfort zone.

MORE COLUMNS

title: 3-star CB Dhillon McGee commits to Penn State
author: Andrew Deal | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State has added another cornerback talent.

Cornerback Dhillon McGee has committed to the Nittany Lions on Wednesday, he announced via Instagram.

McGee is an important addition after former commits Zachary Gleason Jr. and Semajay Robinson both flipped within three days of each other two weeks ago.

The Texas native ranks as the No. 496 player in the country, No. 47 cornerback and No. 67 player in Texas.

McGee becomes the first player in Penn State’s class from Texas, adding to the nationwide approach of the 2027 class.

After getting drafted in the second round of the 2023 NFL Draft, Strange worked his way to the top of the depth chart in Jacksonville, becoming a reliable target for Trevor Lawrence. title: Penn State hockey draft profile | G Ryder Fetterolf’s puck tracking, quick reflexes key to success
author: Ashley Felsberg I The Daily Collegian
description:
Ryder Fetterolf, an 18-year-old Penn State commit, is set to take part in the 2026 NHL Draft. The Sewickley, Pennsylvania native is set to join Guy Gadowsky’s squad in the 2026-2027 season, draft pending.

Fetterolf is coming off of a successful season with the Ottawa 67s in the Ontario Hockey League. He had six shutouts with a .923 save percentage in his 41 games with the 67s this past season; the shutouts set a league record as the most for a rookie in a single OHL season.

Bio & metrics

Hometown: Sewickley, PA

DOB: 1/05/2008

Height: 6-foot

Catches: Left

Position: Goaltender

Juniors statistics

Record: 47-15-3

Shutouts: 6

Goals-against average: 2.07

Save percentage: .923

Awards

CHL Goaltender of the Year

OHL Dinty Moore Trophy

OHL First All-Rookie Team

OHL All-Star Team

OHL Goaltender of the Year

OHL Lowest Team Goals Against Average (Dave Pinkey Trophy)

Projections

Projected round drafted: Mid Round

Player comparison: Jacob Fowler

Scouting report

Standing at 6-foot, he is below the optimal height for NHL goalie prospects and will largely be considered a developmental pick.

Though he’s considered small, scouts have noted that it has little effect on his performance. The young American player is ranked No. 140 by Elite Prospects, No. 84 by Smaht Scouting, and the No. 11 North American goaltender by NHL Central Scouting.

Because of his size, Fetterolf must rely on his other attributes to make up for what his body can’t block. This forces him to hone in on his puck tracking strengths and quick reflexes. While he may not have the technical skill of others in his class, his athleticism makes up for it and his results don’t suffer.

Another struggle for Fetterolf is limiting multiple-chance shots. Coaches like Fetterolf because of his reliability, as his flexibility and refusal to give up on a play allows him to make these second and third stops in a possession.

While he can save these additional opportunities, his time in college athletics will allow him to work on eliminating extra shots for the offense all together.

Best team fit: Edmonton Oilers

Edmonton is looking to draft a goalie but doesn’t appear to be desperate for one just yet. The Oilers have goalies in rotation but are looking toward a long term solution. Because it doesn’t seem urgent, drafting Fetterolf now would allow him to develop his skills and finish growing while he plays at Penn State.

Other teams in a similar situation would include the Buffalo Sabres, and Philadelphia Flyers.

With Fetterolf expected to be drafted between the third to fifth round range, and Edmonton having several mid-round picks, this could be a potential landing place for him.

title: Film review | Penn State LB commit Case Alexander displays patience, powerful tackling
author: Andrew Mercer | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State started the week on the right foot by securing the commitment of 4-star linebacker Case Alexander, who made his pledge on Sunday.

Alexander is ranked as the No. 266 player nationally, No. 22 linebacker and No. 7 player out of Oklahoma, per 247Sports composite. Although he primarily focuses on defense, Alexander also plays tight end like his older brother and future teammate, Cooper, and his father, Stephen, who played eight seasons in the NFL.

He had a strong 2025-26 season with 88 tackles, 10 tackles for loss, five sacks, a forced fumble and a recovery. He helped lead Washington High School to a 12-2 record, ultimately falling in the Oklahoma 2A-I state championship game to Jones High School.

Alexander’s film showcases his patience in waiting for lanes to open up before attacking with thunderous tackles, as well as his ability to cause havoc with elite downhill speed and kick-blocking capabilities on special teams.

Patient, lurking presence

Alexander shows immense patience and discipline to lurk behind the defensive line, seamlessly reading option plays in order to react and make a move on the ball carrier.

When the quarterback drops back to pass, Alexander waits for the right moment to burst through a hole at the line of scrimmage and shoot for a sack.

If no such opportunity appears, Alexander will continue to read the quarterback’s eyes and sit in the passing lanes. Oftentimes, the quarterback will opt for a checkdown pass, which Alexander shuts down before additional yardage can be taken.

When the running back attempts to squeeze past the defense, Alexander is the first to enter the fray, aiding the interior. On outside rushes and screen passes, he pushes away from his opponent to eliminate a potential blocker before progressing to attacking the carrier.

Alexander doesn’t immediately charge in when the quarterback leaves the pocket. He waits to see if his opponent has an open target or if everyone upfield is locked down — only then does he give himself the green light to advance for the sack.

When tackling, Alexander leads with his strong shoulder for maximum power, but doesn’t use blind-side hits that he could be flagged for.

Crafty tackles

When Alexander makes tackles, he spreads his arms wide for the largest radius of effect. When those arms come down, the impact is powerful enough to stun the ball carrier.

In those moments, Alexander uses the force behind his tackles to punch or slap at the ball, sometimes resulting in a fumble. Because he subsequently continues to flatten the opposition, the ball is up for grabs and is often recovered by his teammates.

If he’s not looking to cause a fumble, he’s suplexing the ball carrier to the ground. He wraps his arms around his opponents before using his upper-body strength to lift them off the ground and deliver a hefty slam.

Although his skillful takedowns give him more opportunities, Alexander doesn’t always need it. Sometimes, all it takes is a stiff bump of the shoulder or push.

Blazing downhill speed

As a multi-sport athlete with a track background, Alexander is incredibly fast. He puts this speed on full display whenever he has a free shot at the quarterback.

Alexander ran a 11.51-second 100-meter dash as a sophomore as well as a 23.38 200-meter time in April as a junior. That kind of straight-away speed allows him to close the gap between him and the quarterback in the blink of an eye.

Should running backs move to the outside and pass Alexander, the young linebacker has the acceleration to quickly recover and track down the ball carrier with a trailing tackle.

When he’s far away from the play, Alexander will maneuver himself across the field to contribute and even make a touchdown-saving tackle on a player that cuts through the initial defensive stand.

Alexander also excels on special teams, specifically when defending against field goals. If he catches the kicking side slacking, he punishes them by sprawling out midair to block the ball, using his 6-foot-3 frame to his advantage.

title: Olivia Rodrigo’s ‘you seem pretty sad for a girl so in love’ is a hopeless romantic’s musical paradise
author: Michelle Diamond | News Editor
description:
As a lover of gut-wrenching music that brings me to tears, Olivia Rodrigo’s newest album “you seem pretty sad for a girl so in love” didn’t deliver anything short of a melodic representation of a yearning girl’s heart.

Released almost two weeks ago, Rodrigo’s album brought fans 13 songs, two other familiar voices, a multitude of TikTok trends and a rollercoaster of emotions.

“drop dead”

Released as a single before the album dropped, “drop dead” quickly became popular across social media — and for good reason. With one of the catchier choruses in the album, this song feels like being a teen girl experiencing love for the first time.

“You’re lookin’ like an angel on the Walls of Versailles” is a beautiful lyric that, I think, encapsulates the song’s poetic depiction of love at first sight.

Rating: 9/10

“stupid song”

Another song about longing for someone, “stupid song” feels so emotionally rich, depicting an uproar of feelings as each line in the chorus gets higher. To me, this song conveys how it feels to reach the tipping point of overwhelming emotions, just waiting to explode.

The metaphors make the song feel like a hopeless romantic pouring their heart out in their diary, something I am sure many of Rodrigo’s listeners can relate to (not to call you out).

Rating: 9/10

“honeybee”

This song is my favorite on the album.

“honeybee” is so beautiful and soft, yet so intense at heart. This song feels comforting but never fails to make me tear up with how deep of a love it portrays.

I would be remiss to not mention Conan Gray, another artist whose music pulls all the heart strings (and my favorite), sings back-up vocals toward the end of the song.

Rating: 10/10

“maggots for brains”

While having a catchy tune and rhythm, I don’t like this song as much as the rest. Thus far in the album, everything has been about love and infatuation. This one, on the other hand, feels more like obsession and codependency.

While I understand the notion of not being yourself when who you love is gone, I can’t personally imagine singing about completely losing myself with my car windows down on a summer night. I understand the intentions and logic, but this song just didn’t touch my heart the same way many of the others did.

Even so, I could get behind listening to it on my way to class, taking in the upbeat energy the album needed.

Rating: 6/10

“u + me = <3”

This song didn’t stand out as anything special to me, but I appreciated the feel-good tune in the middle of the album.

This is a semi-compliment, but “u + me = <3” feels like it belongs in a Disney love story between a jock and a nerd.

I don’t find the melody particularly hummable, and the lyrics didn’t captivate me as much as they did in her other songs. I could take or leave this song.

Rating: 4/10

“my way”

This one reminds me of Rodrigo’s first album, “SOUR.” There is an angry and edgy feel that sets this song apart from the rest in its album, and I like how it takes the listener through a whole new emotion than they started with during the first track.

The chorus is a lot of fun and sounds like the perfect choice to yell out when you want to romanticize your anger.

Rating: 7/10

“purple”

Wholesome love song is the only way to describe “purple.”

The use of colors like blue, red, black and, of course, purple gave the song a visual touch. It connected images with emotions, and I found that to be a powerful motif in exploring the simple yet complicated feelings of love.

Art appeals to multiple senses, and I liked how this song relied on imagery to further the abstract ways in which emotions can be felt.

Rating: 8/10

“the cure”

I think everyone can relate to this song on some level. The sentiment of so fiercely grabbing onto someone or something that makes you feel okay only to find out that’s not how healing works is illustrated so beautifully in the lyrics.

To me, this song represents how it feels to feel broken, and the consequent anger that you feel toward yourself after the breaking. I love how this song emphasizes that this kind of isolation is a universal experience while framing mental health as physical health.

Rating: 9/10

“begged”

I would deem this song the “prettiest” of the album. Her voice is so light and gentle as she sings the enchanting tune. I could hear the hurt in her voice, and her emotion translates directly to the listener.

I could listen to this chorus on repeat to embrace “the feels.”

Rating: 10/10

“what’s wrong with me”

Featuring Robert Smith from The Cure, this song captures girlhood.

Incorporating Smith into the track gives the song a unique feel that sounds like what it feels to lay in your bed and contemplate life. Similar to the song “the cure,” this song resonates with those hurting on the inside.

Rating: 10/10

“less”

This song touched my heart from the first note.

“If loving me means letting go and wishing me the best/Then I guess/I wish, I wish, I wish you loved me less” might be the saddest lyrics I’ve heard Rodrigo sing.

This is a phenomenal song that will get listeners in touch with their heart — so long as they have tissues ready.

Rating: 9/10

“expectations”

After the emotional journey that was “less,” Rodrigo knew her fans needed yet another feel-good song.

“expectations” embraces not settling and instead appreciating one’s self-worth. Every girl needs this in her playlist, whether it be for general good vibes or a reminder of all the good she deserves.

Rating: 8/10

“cigarette smoke”

This stunning melody capped off the album on an unsettling yet eerily beautiful note, and the transcendent and freeing feeling the bridge evokes is very worth mentioning.

Ending the entire album with the words “go dark” takes all the pain and intense love Rodrigo depicted through the 13 tracks and packages it up with a bow, much like how many of us handle our similar emotions.

Rating: 8/10

title: Penn State announces date for annual Lift for Life event
author: Andrew Mercer | The Daily Collegian
description:
Uplifting Athletes' signature event is returning to its birthplace soon.

Penn State's Uplifting Athletes chapter announced that its annual "Lift for Life" event will take place at 9 a.m on July 1 at the Lasch Building practice fields.

Uplifting Athletes is a non-profit organization dedicated to supporting people affected by rare diseases. Open to the public, the suggested entry donations are $10 for adults and $5 for kids. All donations go towards funding the rare disease community.

The event first started at Penn State in the summer of 2003 as a weightlifting competition. Since then, Uplifting Athletics has expanded across the United States.

Last year, Penn State football's fundraising goal was $8,000, and the Nittany Lions were successful, raising a total of $8,571.

The Nittany Lions will compete against each other in "strength and conditioning workouts" including the bench press and other athletic challenges, according to the Uplifting Athletes web page. Following the event, the athletes will be open to sign autographs for a 15-minute period.
