title: Which Penn State transfers could have big impacts at their new destinations?
author: Andrew Deal | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State lost nearly half of its players to the transfer portal following the firing of James Franklin, with those players landing at destinations all over the country.

While a group followed Franklin to Virginia Tech, others found fresh homes with new coaches, leading to opportunities for themselves and a chance to make a major impact in a new uniform.

Here’s some of the former Penn State players who could make the biggest impact with their new team.

Ethan Grunkemeyer, quarterback, Virginia Tech

One of the bigger names on the list, Ethan Grunkemeyer, followed Franklin and quarterback coach Danny O’Brien to Virginia Tech to be the starting quarterback.

In Grunkemeyer’s seven games as a starter last season, he continued showing growth, culminating in a career-high 260-yard performance in the Nittany Lions’ Pinstripe Bowl win against Clemson.

Grunkemeyer does have some competition at quarterback in Blacksburg — Franklin added former 4-star North Carolina quarterback Bryce Baker and true freshman Troy Huhn has impressed — but he’s the likely starter for the Hokies’ Week 1 game against VMI.

With a relatively light schedule, Grunkemeyer could produce at a high level in Franklin’s first year at Virginia Tech.

Jaxon Smolik, quarterback, Temple

Another quarterback who transferred out, Jaxon Smolik, stayed in Pennsylvania and transferred to Temple.

While Smolik didn’t see much time with the Nittany Lions, he proved his talent in Temple’s spring game, where he reportedly threw for 256 yards and three touchdowns on an 80% completion percentage.

Smolik was facing his teammates, but with Temple in a conference that has once again opened up due to coaching changes, he could find success with the Owls.

Penn State will see it firsthand in Lincoln Financial Field on Sept. 12, when it takes on Smolik and Temple in Week 2.

Luke Reynolds, tight end, Virginia Tech

Luke Reynolds was highly regarded coming out of high school, ranking as one of the top tight ends in his class and a 4-star recruit to the Nittany Lions. With his potential and the trust he had with Franklin, he could be in for a big season as a familiar early target for Grunkemeyer at Virginia Tech.

Reynolds didn’t live up to expectations in his first year as a starter, where he notched just 257 yards and no touchdowns. With his athleticism and size, Reynolds’ production could take another jump if he proves to be a legitimate weapon for the Hokies’ offense.

TJ Shanahan Jr., offensive lineman, Florida

T.J. Shanahan Jr., one of Penn State’s most consistent offensive linemen from last season, will slot in as one of the guards at Florida after following Phil Trautwein to Gainesville.

With one of the top running backs in the country in Jadan Baugh, Shanahan could get more recognition if he’s able to lead the way for Baugh, building on a strong 2025 with the Nittany Lions.

Amare Campbell, linebacker, Tennessee

Amare Campbell was one of the bigger losses for Penn State in the offseason, as he chose to follow defensive coordinator Jim Knowles and a few other Nittany Lions to Tennessee.

Campbell was a pure ball hawk with the Nittany Lions, totaling 103 tackles. He was the top linebacker and could be the same for the Volunteers.

With the comfortability in Knowles’ defense already, Campbell’s transition to his third team in three years could be easier than this past season, coming with even higher production in the SEC.

A.J. Harris, cornerback, Indiana

A.J. Harris didn’t have a strong year with Penn State last season, but Harris could find himself again with Indiana, the reigning national champions.

Indiana has had a lot of success at cornerback under Curt Cignetti, and Harris has the talent to put it all together with the Hoosiers. He proved his talents in his first year, 2025, with the Nittany Lions — the best season of his collegiate career.

After some roster turnover from the national championship team, Harris could make a major impact with Indiana and get himself back into NFL draft conversations.

Dejuan Lane, safety, Tennessee

Dejuan Lane rounds out the list and is also making his way to Knoxville, joining Campbell and edge rusher Chaz Coleman. While Coleman’s expectations are up in the air right now, Lane could assert himself as a top safety for the Volunteers.

Tennessee’s defense hasn’t necessarily been known to have big time playmakers, but with Knowles now at the helm as the defensive coordinator, a player like Lane could make an immediate impact.

Lane was hyped up during his freshman year, and though he wasn’t talked about as much during his sophomore year, he still had a strong season, giving him an opportunity to continue to grow in a new home.

title: Penn State hockey draft profile | C Yegor Shilov brings high-level offense to next level
author: Ashley Felsberg I The Daily Collegian
description:
Russian native and recent Penn State commit Yegor Shilov is projected as a late first round or early second round pick in the upcoming 2026 NHL Draft. The 18-year-old originally committed to Boston University, but switched to Happy Valley in May,.

He put forth a successful season with the QMJHL’s Victoriaville Tigres. Shilov put up 82 points and 32 goals in the junior hockey league — all within 63 games.

During his time in Quebec, Shilov was noted for his creative play and domination on the power play. Scouts believe he needs a couple of years reshaping in college athletics before moving to the NHL, due to his lack of speed and one-dimensional style of play. However, his impressive shot speed and placement make him a posable threat against goalies.

Shilov’s offensive vision, shifty style of play and creative puck control will allow him to blossom in college play coming out of Juniors.

Bio & metrics

Hometown: Tyumen, Russia

DOB: 4/30/2008

Height: 6-foot-1

Shoots: Left

Position: Center

Juniors statistics

Games Played: 63

Goals: 32

Assists: 50

Points: 82

Playoff statistics

Games Played: 4

Goals: 2

Assists: 3

Points: 5

Awards

QMJHL Offensive Rookie of the Year

QMJHL All Rookie Team

Projections

Projected round drafted: Late first to early second round

Scouting report

Shilov excels offensively but struggles going up against physical players. He lacks confidence when going in for hits and opts to dart from defenders, gearing away from bigger players. He has a strong sense of the ice, but tends to succeed more when the game is slowed down.

Along with his comfortable sense of positioning, he can keep the puck on his stick in highly possessed situations, and can slide in a fast yet accurate shot against skilled goaltenders.

Shilov is a reliable center, winning 54.8% of his faceoffs throughout his time in Victoriaville, while also being ranked the No. 19 North American skater by NHL Central Scouting.

Best team fit: Philadelphia Flyers

The Flyers made the 2026 NHL Playoffs for the first time since 2020, yet suffered an early second-round exit against Carolina’s Hurricanes. The Flyers were shut out in a four game sweep and struggled to score goals, only scoring five times in four games.

The Flyers are a tight knit defensive team, often forcing their opponents to find small holes in the zone. They force players to sit on the outside and clog up shooting lanes.

The team’s main issue is generating shots. A player like Shilov would even out this imbalance, as he mainly prospers in finding tiny holes and placing the puck in those openings.

While it may be two to three years before Shilov skates in a professional rink, a player like him could play a key factor in rebuilding Philadelphia’s offense.

title: Column | Fully coating my tongue in mezcal
author: Lailah Acuña I The Daily Collegian
description:
When most people think of Mexican food, they think of tacos, empanadas, tortas, burritos, quesadillas or elote. What most people don't think about is the culture and science behind the food.

Gastronomy differs from cuisine. While cuisine is an organized system of recipes that are shaped by geography, climate and regional culture, gastronomy focuses more on how food connects to society and history. It explores why and when we eat, like celebrations, cultural events, or familial gatherings.

Throughout the six weeks I’ve spent abroad in Mexico, gastronomy has been a huge part in how I’ve learned about and more deeply appreciated Mexican culture. Oaxaca, Mexico is one of the few places I visited where I was able to learn and experience different gastronomic experiences.

One that especially stuck with me was a visit to a mezcalería. Roughly a majority of mezcal, a traditional Mexican alcoholic beverage, is produced in the southern state of Oaxaca. It’s made from agave, like tequila, but the drinks have key differences; tequila can only be made from Blue Weber agave, but mezcal can be made from over 30 different species of agave.

The process of making agave follows five steps: harvesting, roasting, milling, fermentation and finally distillation. It’s a long process that can take up between two to four weeks — and a plant may require up to 30 years to fully mature.

The practice of making mezcal goes back to pre-hispanic origin. Indigenous civilizations in Mexico associated the agave plant, or maguey, to Mayahuel the Goddess of Fertility. The indigenous peoples discovered that the sap of the agave produced a mildly alcoholic beverage, which they called pulque, when fermented. Following the Spanish conquest, more advanced distillation techniques were introduced to Mexico.

Combining these new techniques and ancestral knowledge, the now more high-proof beverage of mezcal was created. It’s enjoyed worldwide, but its cultural significance is still of great value in Mexico.

When I visited the mezcal farm, one of the workers gave us samples of about seven different mezcals. Depending on the species of agave, aging process, or production method, different mezcal varieties and unique flavors are made.

Mezcal tastes great, but I found the best part of it all to be the experience leading up to actually drinking it. Here’s what I was told: First, you pour a shot glass of the mezcal of your choice. Then, pour a little bit of mezcal into the palm of your hands and rub it together to smell the undertones and aroma of the drink. Next comes the cheers, that of which I learned many. One of the most famous is: “¡Arriba, abajo, al centro, y pa' dentro!” (It’s basically the equivalent of our “bottoms up,” but it directly translates to “up, down, center and inside!”)

Then you drink it, letting it fully coat your tongue, swallowing without opening your mouth, breathing in through your nose — don't open your mouth! — and finally exhaling. It’s usually chased with a slice of orange or citrus.

From what I experienced, mezcal isn’t so much about just having a drink as it is engaging in a tasting ritual. I see it as an appreciation for the flavor and process, as a drink saved for special occasions and gatherings.

But by all means, if you want to mix it, make fun drinks and just have a night, go for it. Just keep in mind that this drink serves more purpose than what meets the eye.

By learning more about the gastronomy of Mexico, I was prompted to think about habits with other drinks and food. How often is what we consume mere muscle memory, mere habit? How often do we slow down to think about the history, significance and process behind what we consume?

I want to be able to answer that with a stronger sense of appreciation for the culture and history behind the food and drink. With this experience, I think I’ve begun to develop that. I want to learn and understand the significance behind more foods and drinks in other cultures, and I’d encourage anyone dissatisfied with their answers to those two questions to do the same.

It’s always important to be knowledgeable in the significance and process behind what you are consuming. Such as for mezcal: Para todo mal, mezcal, para todo bien, también... y si no hay remedio, litro y medio. ¡Salud!

MORE ABROAD COLUMNS

title: Penn State women's basketball earns commitment from international guard Irene Garcia
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Coach Tanisha Wright's squad continues to grow stronger.

Penn State earned a commitment from international guard Irene Garcia on Monday, bringing the Lady Lions up to nine players.

Garcia has one year of collegiate experience in 2024-25, where she played in 18 games with LaSalle. She averaged just 3.8 minutes per game, with seven points and 13 rebounds on the season.

title: Duck Donuts State College location permanently closed
author: Saxtyn Lutz | The Daily Collegian
description:
After eight years of business, Duck Donuts at State College closed its doors for the final time on Sunday.

“After many wonderful years serving the State College area, our shop will close on Sunday,” the company said in a Facebook post on Sunday evening. “We are incredibly grateful for the loyal guests, families, and community members who supported our shop. Thank you for your support, your visits, and your love for warm, delicious donuts.”

The location didn’t immediately respond to requests for comment, but the Duck Donuts at State College Facebook page is listed as “permanently closed.”

Tucked away in the back left corner of the Martin Luther King Jr. Plaza on 125 South Fraser Street, Duck Donuts sold warm doughnuts with various icing flavors and toppings.

They served seasonal doughnut flavors such as Lemon Lime Pie and Mango Tajin Donut, as well as fan favorites including Blueberry Pancake and Maple Bacon. Additionally, they served lattes, mochas, hot chocolate, lemonades, donut ice cream sandwiches, donut ice cream sundaes and donut-topped milkshakes.

The franchise has closed multiple locations over the last year including Mechanicsburg, Pennsylvania; Ammon, Idaho; Ashburn and Lynchburg, Virginia; Selden, New York and East Cobb, Georgia.

Despite this, Duck Donuts remains a popular donut chain with 120 locations across the country and 12 remaining locations in Pennsylvania. Duck Donuts Hershey is now the closest location to State College.

title: 6 books, movies to experience before freshman year
author: Ava Krysko | The Daily Collegian
description:
The anticipation of returning to school can accompany summer’s long stretches. For high school graduates, your first year of college is on the horizon, and it’s without a doubt an exciting yet nerve-racking time.

Now that you’ve graduated and the flurry of end-of-year senior activities is over, you have time to work on the list of Netflix shows and movies you've been dying to see, and maybe even incorporate some reading into your newfound downtime.

After all, you could be starting your next chapter — both in life and in your media consumption. Here’s six different books and movies that will start your college career off on the right foot and, hopefully, get you excited for the years to come.

Books

1. “Perks of Being a Wallflower” by Stephen Chbosky

One of the most prominent coming-of-age trope examples, “The Perks of Being a Wallflower” explores Charlie, who embarks on a journey to make friends and leave his comfort zone.

As your high school days wrap up, it’s the perfect, relatable read for those looking for something that reminisces on the end of adolescence and showcases feelings like anxiety and uncertainty.

While this book does have plot twists, mature content and themes, it’s an in-depth, insightful read on self-discovery and teenage friendships.

2. “The Catcher in the Rye” by JD Salinger

Of course, you can’t leave out “The Catcher in the Rye” when it comes to books that exemplify growing up and changing as a person — I mean, it basically created the genre.

Although you may have read this already (and it often comes across as outdated), it’s the exact kind of book that fits the theme of transitioning to adulthood.

Holden, the main character, takes readers on a journey of reflection, both on his life and what kind of person he wants to be. Holden can come off as a bit of a spoiled jerk at times, which is usually a turn off for readers. But, if you’re interested in a coming-of-age story that’s still a classic, this may be the book for you.

3. “Normal People” by Sally Rooney

For all the romance fans, Sally Rooney’s "Normal People” is a great recommendation. That is, if you love incessant pining and miscommunication with a dash of unrequited love.

Either way, “Normal People” perfectly fits as a novel, because it follows Marianne and Connell through the end of their high school years and into college as they figure out who they are.

Throughout the story, both of them learn what love is, and deal with all of the hardships that come with that lesson. Not to mention the rockiness of their relationship lingers throughout the entire book — so there’s enough drama that you’ll want to stay up until 3 a.m. to keep reading.

Movies

1. “Monsters University”

Maybe this one isn’t the most scholarly pick, but when it comes to movies you should watch before freshman year, this one certainly has merit.

It follows familiar characters Mike and Sully as they navigate their freshmen year of college, and flawlessly depicts all the different aspects of what college is like (well, without all of the monsters, of course).

It’s a fun watch that will get you excited for the college lifestyle, and hopefully ward off any of those last-minute nerves. After all, who can resist the temptation of seeing Mike and Sully’s best friend origin story?

2. “Ferris Bueller’s Day Off”

If you haven’t already watched it, you definitely should. “Ferris Bueller’s Day Off” is basically the pinnacle of end-of-high-school movies. It follows Ferris, his girlfriend and his best friend as they skip a school day to roam around Chicago.

Heartfelt yet comical, this movie will help you think about your own feelings about leaving high school and get you ready to take on your next journey.

3. “Lady Bird”

Starring Saoirse Ronan, "Lady Bird” explores the relationship between a mother and her daughter as the latter transitions from high school to college.

As the name suggests, the movie follows Lady Bird during her senior year of high school, a teenager sick of her small town, ready to move on to college. When she eventually goes to college, the feelings she has are more bittersweet than she once thought they would be.

This movie is for those who have some trepidation about starting a new experience, but are doing it anyway.

No matter which of these selections you pick, it’s important to remember your college experience is entirely your own, and will be what you make it. So, after checking out these titles, be sure to enter your first year with a positive mindset.

title: Advocacy, tutoring, healthcare | Student resources available on campus
author: Annabella Chaklos I The Daily Collegian
description:
For many new Penn State students, the excitement of such a large campus can be exhilarating, overwhelming or nerve-wracking, leaving incoming scholars feeling lost or unaware of all Penn State has to offer. Whether students are struggling to afford basic needs, require extra tutoring, or want new clothes, Penn State has resources for just about anything a student needs.

Here is a list of useful resources for both new and current students alike to benefit from during their time at Penn State.

Student Care and Advocacy Center

The Student Care and Advocacy Center, located at 222 Boucke, focuses on setting students up with resources.

It has information on important aid students can receive for tuition, expenses, housing, food and health care. The center also provides students with basic needs such as clothing, toiletries and other items during their business hours which are Monday through Friday 8:00 a.m. to 5:00 p.m.

Students can also fill out a Basic Needs Request Form for themselves or on behalf of another student, and the center will follow-up with a case worker who will give personalized guidance.

Their website can also be found here.

University Health Services (UHS)

UHS, located at the Student Health Center on Eisenhower Road, is where students can find medical appointments, vaccines, pharmaceutical services, ultrasounds, x-rays, lab work, physical therapy and ambulance services.

Students are required to submit their insurance information before the start of each academic year or they can purchase the Student Health Insurance Plan.

Appointments can be made here.

Counseling and Psychological Services (CAPS)

CAPS is Penn State’s mental health services. Located on the fifth floor of the Student Health Center, CAPS has self-care opportunities, individualized counseling, group counseling and psychiatric care.

To begin counseling, CAPS will perform an over-the-phone screening to decide what services will be most beneficial for students. Students may then be offered two rounds of six-week individual counseling.

CAPS also holds free, 30-minute, same-day counseling in various places on campus or virtually called CAPS Chat. Crisis services are also offered at the 24/7 Penn State Crisis Line at 1-877-229-6400.

Penn State Learning

Penn State Learning is the university’s free, peer-led tutoring and learning services, offering guided study groups, tutoring and workshops in fifteen different subjects, including math, writing and seven languages.

The Writing Center in 220 Boucke holds writing and world language tutoring; 7 Sparks holds math and undergraduate public speaking tutoring; and W102 Pattee Library in Sidewater Commons holds writing and peer research tutoring. Other subject tutoring is held virtually or by appointment via Starfish.

Professional Attire Closet & Professional Headshots

The Professional Attire Closet and Professional Headshot services are offered by Career Services and run in partnership with University Park Fee Board and University Park Undergraduate Association.

Career Services offers free, professional clothing for networking events, career fairs and interviews. Students get one professional outfit per academic year. It also has a photography studio where students can get free, professional headshots taken.

Found in the Bank of America Career Services Center and available Monday through Friday from 10:00 a.m. to 4:00 p.m, no appointment is needed for either service — only a valid student ID.

R-VOICE Center

The R-VOICE Center is a resource for students who have experienced sexual assault, harassment, dating violence, domestic violence or stalking. It also provides reproductive health resources, like free contraceptives.

The R-VOICE Center is in 222U Boucke and is open Monday through Friday from 8:00 a.m. to 5:00 p.m.

Center for Sexual and Gender Diversity (CSGD)

CSGD, located in LL011 HUB-Robeson Center from 8:00 a.m. to 5:00 p.m., provides education on resources for LGBTQ+ students. It also has identity-based programs, helps students access gender-affirming care and inclusive housing and can help with reporting misconduct, bias or harassment.

It also has the Clothing Transit, allowing queer students to exchange or obtain clothing for free.

Student Disability Resources

This resource office helps students with disabilities receive access to accommodations within their classes and overall campus experience. Students can file discrimination complaints, learn more about accommodations by their course and different scholarship information on their website or in their office.

Student Disability Resources, located at 116 Boucke, is available Monday through Friday from 8:00 a.m. to 5:00 p.m.

The Sokolov-Miller Financial and Life Skills Center

The Sokolov-Miller Financial and Life Skills Center allows students, faculty, staff and alumni schedule free appointments with financial coaches to learn how to manage finances independently.

It provides individual coaching, presentations, mentoring programs, workshops, self-study modules and webinars. This center can be found in 11-A Grange Building or can be contacted at finlit@psu.edu.

The Pattee Media Commons

The Pattee Media Commons are located in the Pattee Library in rooms W131-W142 and are open Monday through Friday from 9:00 a.m. to 5:00 p.m.

The commons provide free creative resources to students by appointment, including a 40-seat iMac computer lab, recording studios with podcast and editing equipment, a video recording studio, 3D print farm finishing and paint stations, an invention studio, a cricut maker, dye sublimation, a sewing machine, sticker printing and a laser engraver.

title: Recruiting roundup | 2028 search, recruiting battles highlight Penn State’s recent news
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State’s recruiting news was minimal last week, but plenty happened behind the scenes.

With the Nittany Lions’ official visit weekends wrapped up, Penn State targets headed elsewhere to continue their recruiting journeys, while coaches began their pursuit of the Class of 2028.

Here’s a look at Penn State’s recruiting action from this week.

Wide receiver situation

Two weeks ago, it appeared as though Penn State would land 4-star receivers Khalil Taylor and Deshawn Hall, adding to a strong class with fellow 4-stars Jamir Dean and Landon Blum already committed.

Instead, there’s a chance the Nittany Lions could have just one wide receiver in the Class of 2027. Dean flipped his commitment to Georgia on June 15 after a month-long battle with the Bulldogs, and Taylor and Hall are trending away from Happy Valley.

Taylor, the No. 13 receiver in his class, had his official visit to Nebraska last weekend. There, he sported a black jersey in photoshoots while he and the visitor group played basketball and sang karaoke with coach Matt Rhule.

Rhule previously stated in a press conference that the black jersey was reserved for committed players during recruiting weekends. But multiple recruits this cycle have worn black jerseys on visits that weren’t committed.

While Hall didn’t take any additional visits this past weekend, he’s continued to trend toward Auburn, with several outlets predicting a commitment to the Tigers. He’s expected to announce his commitment Tuesday.

Recruiting battles

Penn State picked up a huge recruiting battle win when Case Alexander committed on Sunday. The 4-star linebacker chose the Nittany Lions over staying in-state with Oklahoma, marking a big pickup for Matt Campbell and company.

Alexander's brother, Cooper, is currently a redshirt sophomore tight end for Penn State, so the two will be reuniting in Happy Valley barring any flips or transfers.

On the uncommitted side of things, all eyes have been on 3-star Dhillon McGee as the next cornerback option after two flips at the position last week. The No. 48 cornerback made his official visit to Texas last weekend, rounding out his top three of Penn State, the Longhorns and SMU.

Joining McGee at Texas was 3-star tackle Lucas Rhoa, who was also in Happy Valley the same time as McGee. Last weekend concluded Rhoa’s official visits, with trips to Oregon, UCLA, Penn State and Texas.

Linebacker Tyson Washington visited Virginia Tech this weekend. Washington was part of Penn State’s big recruiting weekend on June 5, but he also has his eyes on James Franklin, Syracuse and Virginia.

Class of 2028

On June 15, coaches were allowed to start interacting with the 2028 class, starting the next era of recruiting for the Nittany Lions.

Among the most significant action from the first week of recruitment was the commitment of kicker Carter Petri. The New York native received an offer on Wednesday after participating in a camp at Penn State and committed three days later.

In addition to the camp during the week, Penn State hosted a recruiting barbeque for the Class of 2028 over the weekend, which included many of the top names in the class.

The Nittany Lions’ first commit in the class, Deonte Flemings Jr., was in attendance for the barbecue. Flemings committed on Jan. 31 and has been active on campus since, including an appearance at Blue-White Practice.

Penn State hosted a pair of top-ranked edge rushers over the weekend, including George Parkinson IV and Cam Toomey. Parkinson IV is the No. 14 edge rusher in his class and No. 2 player from Pennsylvania, while Toomey is the No. 9 edge rusher and No. 1 player from New York.

Arguably the biggest name on campus was 5-star cornerback Nasir Richardson, who had received an offer on Jan. 31. Richardson is one of the top players in the class, ranked No. 13 overall and is the top cornerback nationally.

title: The ‘dos' and ‘don'ts' of Penn State football for new students
author: Andrew Mercer | The Daily Collegian
description:
Welcome to Penn State, young football fans.

Happy Valley brings together students from all backgrounds, many of whom share the same love — sports. However, attending a football game at Beaver Stadium is a different experience than the majority of other sports at Penn State.

Here are the Dos and Don’ts that you should be aware of going into your first football season.

Do — Arrive early

You’d be surprised how fast the student section fills up to maximum capacity.

If you want your spot among the students, and don’t want to miss the pregame theatrics, you should arrive at the stadium no later than 30 minutes before kickoff. The Blue Band will play, the Lionettes will dance and the anticipation builds over that time. You especially wouldn’t want to miss the Floating Lions routine.

For bigger games, like the Big Ten opener or the White Out, try to arrive at least an hour and a half prior to kickoff. Beat the chaos and get some snacks while the bulk of the crowd is outside tailgating.

If you’re early enough, you can be a part of the iconic S-Zone, where the Lion Ambassadors hand out specific shirts to form an “S” in the center of the student section. It’s the easiest way to look back at the game online and see where exactly you were sitting.

It’s worth noting that Nittanyville, the student organization behind the student section, gets the first few rows of the bleachers while everyone else files in above them.

Don’t — Leave early

Conversely, you shouldn’t leave the game early, no matter what the outcome of the game is.

If the game is within two scores, don’t go anywhere — you may be about to witness a legendary comeback or nail-biting end to the game. If that happens while you’re on your way back to your dorm hall, you’d regret it.

Even if the game is a complete blowout, favoring either team, there is still much tradition to take part in. This includes the singing of the alma mater and the ringing of the victory bell should the Nittany Lions come out on top.

These are things that bring the community together. Swaying back and forth with your friends or strangers singing the alma mater will become a core memory. Sometimes it’s rowdy after a win or somber after a tough loss but it’s a piece of Penn State that bridges the connection from the past to the present.

If you leave early, you’d miss out.

Do — Learn the songs and chants

It seems like everybody knows the famous “We Are Penn State” chant, but that’s not the only one that you should know by heart.

There are many variations of the “We Are” chant that plays in the stadium, and at specific times too. When the Nittany Lions run out onto the field or snag a turnover, “Zombie Nation” by Kernkraft 400 blasts over the loudspeakers with the fans filling the gap between the beat with the famous slogan.

The Blue Band will also play a slowed-down version of the song at various intervals, with a fittingly slowed-down chant to accompany it.

Right before kickoff in the first and second halves, a remix of “Circle of Life” from The Lion King plays. Students, who are given shakers upon entering the arena, hold them horizontally as if mimicking Rafiki holding up Simba.

In addition, it’s important to remember the “Let’s Go P-S-U” chant. When the Blue Band starts playing, follow other’s leads when they raise their arms and when they put them down, clapping on beat when the music starts back up again.

That brings us to the two harder songs to learn: “Fight On’ State” and the alma mater.

“Fight On’ State” plays immediately after Penn State scores a touchdown, pausing midway through so that the kicker can make his extra-point attempt, and subsequently finishing. The alma mater plays immediately following the end of the game, where the team salutes the fans before exiting through the tunnel.

Both songs are also prevalent at the vast majority of other Penn State sporting events, including hockey, wrestling and volleyball, so it’s important to know each of these for the duration of your time in Happy Valley.

Don’t — “Sit” in the student section

You don’t sit in the student section, you stand. Even between plays, during timeouts and between quarters, the students behind the end zone don’t actually use the bleachers until halftime.

It’s a long, exhausting day, as you’re standing for roughly four hours straight with small breaks in between to use the bathroom if needed. However, the constant standing is what brings the energy to the crowd — voices are amplified and energized.

Besides, if you sit down, you won’t be able to see the action because the taller folks standing in front of you will be blocking the view.

Do — Wear Penn State gear (and know where to find some)

This should go without saying, but you never know.

Fans will generally be wearing blue and white in some fashion, whether it be Penn State’s uniforms, the striped jumpsuits or a plain outfit that fits the scheme. Plan to wear a lot of white, though, as the Stripe Out, Helmet Stripe and of course, the White Out all require the student section to wear white.

With that said, there have been sightings of fans putting in the effort to honor a past alumnus by wearing their NFL jersey. While the sentiment is there, make sure to check the schedule — and the colors — of Penn State’s opponent that week. Therefore, you wouldn’t want to show up to Beaver Stadium wearing a Saquon Barkley Philadelphia Eagles jersey for the Week 1 game against Marshall.

You may be confused for an enemy; if you do end up wearing that, just don’t sit in the student section. Sit anywhere else for your own safety.

Well, you may ask what if you don’t own any Penn State drip? In that case, The Family Clothesline is located right on East College Ave and has everything you need to look at home on your first game day.

Don’t — Tailgate too hard

You paid a lot of money for those tickets, whether it be for the student section or for a seat elsewhere in the stadium. That’s why it’s imperative that you stay energized and coherent for the main event: the game itself.

Tailgating is huge in college football overall, but especially at Penn State. Hot dogs and burgers on the grill, cornhole and pong on plastic tables, loud music on speakers. It’s all part of the festivities.

But it can be overwhelming and it can drain all of your energy fast if you aren’t mindful about it. Make sure to pace yourself as there’s a lot of screaming, singing and dancing in the stadium during the game too.

For those who are over the age of 21, drink responsibly. You want to be able to understand what’s going on in the game, and remember it too. Safety is of the utmost importance. So, don’t go too crazy, no matter how much fun it’ll be out in the lots.

title: Juneteenth celebrations unite State College residents, visitors at annual block party
author: Justin Elliott | The Daily Collegian
description:
State College residents took to the 100th block of South Fraser Street on Saturday afternoon to cap off Juneteenth weekend with an annual block party. The event was filled with music, poetry, dancing, food and more.

This was the town’s seventh year formally celebrating Juneteenth, the U.S. federal holiday commemorating the official end of the country’s slavery in 1865. The event followed Friday’s Juneteenth Art Exhibition and was put together by the Borough of State College, Penn State’s Center for the Performing Arts and the Happy Valley Adventure Bureau.

Hundreds of attendees navigated the block party, which stretched from the intersection of South Fraser Street and East College Avenue to the intersection of Fraser and West Beaver Avenue. The street was lined with tents offering books, posters, food, merchandise and accessories. Among the tents, there was a voting registration stand.

Kids in face paint and adults in Juneteenth shirts explored each stop.

The theme for this year was “Roots, Rhythm, and Resilience: Celebrating Our Legacy.” State College Mayor Ezra Nanes gave an opening speech.

“Juneteenth is a celebration of freedom, of resilience, culture, family, creativity and community,” Nanes said.

Joan Bouchard, a retired professor, said she was impressed by the energy at the event.

“This is the most inspiring thing they’ve done all year,” Bouchard said about the borough’s efforts.

Penn State student Morgan Parker performed the Black national anthem, “Lift Every Voice and Sing.”

Julian Washington, a Penn State graduate and New Jersey native, said he appreciated the diversity of kind and respectful people on the block.

“They make it feel like a family environment,” Washington said.

The poem segment of the event was kicked off by State College’s official poet laureate, Carmin Wong, who performed “Liberation Rhythm.” Social justice advocate Shih-In Ma performed “Spirit of Ghana,” which followed her trip to the country nearly five decades ago.

The final speech was a reenactment by retired professor Charles Dumas and friends, dressed in Union soldier uniforms. The reenactment featured an American flag and was dedicated to the African American Union soldiers and other freedom fighters who helped win the Civil War.

David Hutchinson, a State College resident who previously worked in Penn State’s classroom technology department, described the event as “perfect.”

“Locally, it’s a tremendous opportunity to get together with people,” he said.

A dance trio featuring women in matching attire with Nigerian and Ugandan flags took the stage to commence the performance segment of the event, moving in unison to hip-hop music. Members of the Unity Church followed with a soulful gospel set as members of the audience swayed with outreached hands.

Two groups of young performers, the Jump DC Superstars and the rayDiANCE Dance Company, electrified the block with unique dance performances featuring jump ropes and matching outfits.

Bubble Gum Soul, a mother-daughter group, brought back classic hits from Michael Jackson and The Jackson 5, such as “I Want You Back” and “Billie Jean” in their tribute performance. Jackie Brown, lead singer and mother to group members Destiny and Diana, changed outfits three times during her Michael Jackson impression.

“We’ve just been inspired by all the African Americans who, through the ages, overcame struggles,” Brown, a Philadelphia native who recently got her bachelor’s degree in marketing through Penn State World Campus, said.

More classic R&B and hip-hop filled the air as award-winning singer and songwriter Maya Azucena performed a set of songs from artists including Jill Scott and Erykah Badu. Performances from The Whew Yeahs, a girl-group that wore 90s-inspired afro wigs, and Eric Ian Farmer & Friends, a long-time local band, kept the music going as it began to rain.

“For me, (the block party) means the human race coming together,” Washington said.

title: Top moments from Penn State track and field’s 2026 outdoor season
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State’s 2026 track season wrapped up last weekend with 10 athletes at the NCAA Championship, capping off a historic season.

Over the past three months, the Nittany Lions set 36 new marks in the program’s top-10 record list, set five school records and earned the blue and white’s first NCAA medal since 2024.

Here’s a look at some of the best moments that contributed to Penn State’s historic 2026 outdoor season.

Hayley Kitching’s 1:57.65

It had been two years since the women’s 800-meter school record was last set. In a matter of two weeks, Hayley Kitching broke it three times.

Kitching’s previous school record of 2:00.70 was broken in the NCAA East First Round, when she ran 1:59.60 for her first collegiate outdoor sub-two-minute race. Two days later in the finals, she ran 1:59.15, earning her spot in the NCAA Championship.

While Kitching didn’t record a school record during the prelims at the championships, she set herself up for a top showdown between herself and Arkansas’ Sanu Jallow.

On June 13, the Coffs Harbour, Australia, native took the line and dropped the No. 2 800m time in NCAA history, running 1:57.65. Kitching took home silver at the NCAA Championship and broke her previous school record by 1.5 seconds.

Ajani Dwyer’s back-to-back sub-10 seconds

Coming into the season, Penn State’s men’s 100-meter record stood at 10.15 seconds, set by Cheickna Traore in 2024.

In the first race of the season, sophomore Ajani Dwyer ran 9.96 seconds, but was wind-aided and not eligible for the record. A week later, he broke the record for the first time in 10.06 seconds, earning his second school record alongside the 60-meter mark.

At the Big Ten Championships, the Sewell, New Jersey, native furthered his success with a time of 10.04 seconds and almost broke it again two days later when he ran a wind-aided 9.93 seconds.

Dwyer officially broke through at the NCAA East First Round, running 9.98 seconds in the prelims for his third 100m school record of the season. Two days later, he ran under 10 seconds again, clinching a spot in the NCAA Championships with a 9.99-second performance.

Nolan Carey’s freshman season success

It’s been nearly a decade since the Nittany Lions have had success in the men’s javelin throw, when Olympian Michael Shuey set the school record with a 79.91-meter throw. Outside of that, the next-best mark was nearly six meters shorter than Shuey’s.

That was, until freshman Nolan Carey took the field as one of Penn State’s only true freshmen this year. Through four of his first five meets of the season, Carey finished second overall, recording a best mark of 67.14 meters.

Carey’s highlight moment came at the Big Ten Championship, where he threw 74.72 meters — good for second in Penn State history.

The Corry, Pennsylvania, native threw for 71.67 meters with his first attempt, which momentarily had him at seventh in school history. After throwing 69.44 meters with his second throw, he set the No. 2 mark in school history with his final attempt.

Jake Palermo’s 400m mark

While now-sophomore Jake Palermo broke the indoor 400-meter record in his second collegiate 400m race, it took him nine attempts to crack the outdoor mark.

The Rochester, New York, native worked his way from seventh in school history to No. 4 during his freshman season, sitting at a 45.75-second personal best. When Year 2 rolled around, he improved his No. 4 mark before ultimately tying Brady Dehret’s third-best time in school history.

In a very fast race against a trio of SEC runners, Palermo set a new school record in 45.19 seconds, breaking a 51-year-old record set by Mike Sands in 1975. While he didn’t earn a spot in the NCAA Championship, he made his mark with both 400m school records as a sophomore.

Men’s 800m dominance

While Penn State had a smaller 800m class with Handal Roban redshirting the outdoor season, the rest of the pack set several top marks across the season.

Graduate student Allon Clay led the way for the Nittany Lions with a Big Ten title and fourth place finish at the NCAA Championship. During the finals at the NCAA East First Round, he set the No. 3 mark in school history in 1:44.63, winning three out of four postseason races to that point.

Fellow NCAA Championship finals qualifier Niko Schultz also worked his way up the all-time rankings with the No. 4 time in school history set at the Virginia Challenge in 1:45.24. The Nebraska transfer earned bronze at the Big Ten Championship and finished sixth at the NCAA Championship.

North Carolina transfer Andrew Regnier and graduate student Yukichi Ishii rounded out the changes in Penn State’s top 10 all-time list. Regnier ran 1:46.10 during the Big Ten Championship prelims for the eighth-best time in school history, while Ishii ran 1:46.21 at the Virginia Challenge for the No. 10 mark.

Hayley Kitching’s 1,500m record

Before Kitching was able to make history in the 800m, she broke her fourth school record in program history.

At the Virginia Challenge in April, Kitching competed in her third collegiate 1,500-meters, running 4:05.81.

The senior mid-distance star broke an eight-year-old record by nearly five seconds, set by Danae Rivers in 2018.

title: 40 year anniversary special | Awarding Penn State’s 1986 championship team
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State’s 1986 national championship-winning season produced a handful of awards for the Nittany Lions, most significantly the team’s second national championship trophy.

Along the way, D.J. Dozier was given First Team All-America honors and was the co-Fiesta Bowl MVP. Three other players who also earned All-America honors.

While those were the national awards handed out to the Nittany Lions, here’s who I’d hand out team awards to.

MVP & Offensive Player of the Year: D.J. Dozier, running back

After rushing for over 700 yards with four touchdowns in 1985, Dozier turned things up during Penn State’s championship run.

On a run-heavy team, Dozier was the leading rusher, as he averaged 73.7 yards per game. He tallied 811 total yards on the season, accounting for over 30% of Penn State’s total rushing yards.

However, it was Dozier’s scoring that took him to the next level and ultimately earns him the title of MVP. The star running back scored twice as many touchdowns as the next-best player, recording 10 touchdowns on the ground and two in the air.

Dozier capitalized in the passing game as well, hauling in 287 yards, the third-most on the team.

One of Dozier’s biggest plays of the season came in the fourth quarter of the Fiesta Bowl against Miami, where he ran the ball six yards into the endzone for the go-ahead score.

Defensive Player of the Year: Duffy Cobbs, defensive back

A key part of Penn State’s championship team came from the defense, which ranked among the best in the nation. The Nittany Lions allowed just 11.2 points per game, the third fewest in the country.

Defensive back Duffy Cobbs played a big role in the defense’s domination, leading the team with four interceptions on the year.

While Cobbs never came away with a score on any of his interceptions, a few of the takeaways were momentum-shifters in big games. In the Homecoming game against Syracuse, Cobbs intercepted the ball on the first play of the game, leading to a 42-3 Penn State domination.

Cobbs also picked off Heisman trophy winning quarterback Vinny Testaverde in the Fiesta Bowl — one of Penn State’s five interceptions against Testaverde — helping clinch the national championship.

Most Improved Player: John Shaffer, quarterback

John Shaffer entered his second year in the starting lineup in 1986 following a less-than-ideal season as a starter. In 1985, he threw for eight touchdowns and 10 interceptions with just a 45.2% completion mark.

Things got better in his second season as he helped lead the Nittany Lions to the national championship.

While Shaffer only added one more touchdown from the year before, he cut his turnover ratio down with just four interceptions. He threw for 1,510 yards on the season, averaging over 130 yards per game.

Assistant of the Year: Fran Ganter

After leading Penn State’s offense as a running back from 1967-70, Fran Ganter returned to lead the offense once again, but this time as offensive coordinator and running backs coach.

While his offense scored 29.6 points per game, his running back room contributed to a majority of Penn State’s national championship-winning season. Ganter coached five running backs who each had over 50 carries, including Tim Manoa, Blair Thomas and Dozier, all three of which had over 500 yards on the season.

Of Penn State’s 38 touchdowns in 1986, the running backs accounted for 26 of them. Following Dozier’s 12 touchdowns was Thomas, who had five touchdowns on the ground.

Ganter’s success leading the offense was on display with over 240 rushing yards per game, showcasing Penn State’s dominant rushing attack.

title: Planning commission recommends rejection of Addison Court redevelopment height amendment
author: Jonathon Chiu | The Daily Collegian
description:
Correction: A previous version of this article had stated that PrimeCore was attempting to obtain LIHTC tax credits from the borough. PrimeCore is attempting to obtain from the borough a text amendment to the height limitations around Addison Court for the construction of Encore. The Daily Collegian apologizes for this error.

The State College Planning Commission voted 3-2 to recommend the State College Borough Council deny PrimeCore its requested Addison Court redevelopment height amendment on Thursday.

Addison Court, located on 120 E. Beaver Ave, opened in 1994 to provide affordable housing to elderly residents and residents with disabilities downtown. PrimeCore Properties, which took over the building’s ownership from Beaver Court Associates, plans to demolish the building to build a mixed-use structure called Encore.

On April 3, PrimeCore requested an amendment to Addison Court’s height limitations, set by borough ordinance 1597 at 65 feet, to better match Encore’s appearance to the Pugh Centre next door, which was developed by PrimeCore. Addison Court was built prior to ordinance 1597’s enactment on June 17, 1999 and is approximately 75 feet tall.

During the planning commission’s May 21 meeting, PrimeCore CEO Ara Kervandjian said it’s pursuing a preservation plan for Addison Court over Encore’s construction. This would include restoring the building’s status within the Low Income Housing Tax Credit (LIHTC) program, which expired on Dec. 31, 2024, to maintain Addison Court’s affordability for tenants.

Kervandjian also said that construction of Encore would commence, should efforts to reobtain placement in LIHTC fail at the meeting.

State College Planning Director Ed LeClear said competition for LIHTC status may make this outcome likely, regardless of what recommendation the planning commission makes on the height amendment request.

LeClear said the borough is willing to assist PrimeCore in obtaining placement in LIHTC, but such assistance wouldn’t guarantee that would happen.

LeClear also added that Kervandjian indicated “yes” when questioned if Addison Court would still be demolished for a new project even if the bid for the height amendment was unsuccessful.

Addison Court Resident Kathryn Santoro said in a public comment that a recommendation to approve the height amendment could set a new precedent for real estate firms like Continental Real Estate — which currently manages Addison Court — to pursue raising its own buildings.

She said the housing increase for students downtown is increasing dependence on the university for business in the borough, while also threatening to further displace State College residents. She added that she is concerned Encore will primarily cater to students.

“We have enough high risers. If Penn State needs housing, let them build it on their side of town,” Santoro said. “There used to be a line between Penn State and State College, and the line has been crossed by Penn State and developers to come in and knock out the residents. The students are gone right now — town is dead.”

Centre County Democratic Socialists Member Sylus Smith said the height amendment allowing developers to build taller buildings without needing to account for community impacts would undermine the borough’s comprehensive zoning ordinance.

“There are things such as underground parking in many of these plots that has been included so that you can get a taller building so that things like congestion can be reduced when we build taller buildings,” Smith, a fourth-year studying political science, said. “If you were to give an out to that, all that would essentially be doing is increasing the benefits for one property owner at the expense of the collective communal interest embodied in the comprehensive rewrite.”

While Smith approves of increased housing in State College, he said, it should be done with respect to the borough’s interests — not those of the developer.

“If the commission thinks that this entire area needs to be upzoned, that's their prerogative. But there's simply no reason to grant one exception to one man when we've already decided that this is what best benefits the community,” Smith said. “I hope you stick by that and, in doing so, I think you will do your small part to keep State College a place that all of us can afford, wherever we're from, however much we make.”

title: Off the record | you + me = confused
author: Miss Behave I The Daily Collegian
description:
Editor’s Note: Off the record is a weekly column written by a fourth-year Collegian staffer under an alias. It aims to promote a space for honest conversations about love, sex and relationships. Those interested in sharing their experiences or submitting a topic can do so through this anonymous form.

Dear Readers,

Unless you live under a rock, you’ve probably seen that Olivia Rodrigo has released her third studio album, “you seem pretty sad for a girl so in love.”

The 12-track album takes listeners through the emotional roller coaster that is falling in love and, hardest of all, staying in love.

Rodrigo’s song “u+me = <3” features the line: “They say modern love's a cruel endeavor. And to that, I say, "F*** it, whatever."

Just like any other 20-something-girl, Rodrigo nails the internal feeling of wanting to commit to someone, hoping it blossoms into something amazing, while also wanting to remain single. (You know, in a chic, empowered way.)

As little girls, we’re told Prince Charming is going to enter our lives and we’ll live happily ever after. But as we grow up, we realize that’s not happening. Instead, we’re stuck with the Sigma Chi frat guy who’s majoring in business and is currently working at his dad’s finance firm.

This type isn’t a huge fan of commitment, and might make you feel like you’re asking for the impossible, wanting more than an “r u up” text at 2 a.m. You might feel like you’re crazy for wanting to be more than a name on Snapchat, like you're in some sort of psychosis after you realize your late-night hook-up is just a daily occurrence for them.

If you can do casual, friends with benefits, a situationship, I’m in awe of you. Truly. From someone who could develop a crush after one conversation, being able to date around and leave your emotions at the door is something I envy.

To the girls, who are content with casual, keep doing it. If men can have a new partner every couple of weeks and brag to all their friends, you can do the same.

If everything mentioned above sounds like a total nightmare, then I get that, too. You should be able to be in a relationship and not worry that they’re going to leave you to pursue someone that is perceived to be “easier” or “better.” Or because, for some reason, they have it ingrained in their brains that they need to sleep around to have the college experience.

You deserve to be able to seek out a relationship full of love and emotional safety, and I don’t know how many times I can emphasize that it might not be in your 9 a.m. class, or at Doggie’s, or in the town of State College at all.

It’s scary to open yourself up to being hurt or taken advantage of, but there’s no growing and falling in love without it. You should be able to express vulnerability and confidence without your partner feeling threatened by qualities that they don’t possess within themselves.

The casual relationship has no strings attached and that’s its own kind of beautiful. Not every romantic partner is your soulmate, and the quicker you realize that people are going to come and go, the better off you're going to be.

And if you become attached, that’s OK too. You’re only human. There’s no point in beating yourself up over feelings that are bound to make their way to the surface eventually.

You deserve grace while dating, hooking up, or doing whatever you want, free of judgment.

I’m also obligated to tell you that safety doesn't go out the door just because you might not see them again. If something feels right, it probably is, but the same is true for the gut feeling that tells you to leave. Use protection, and remember that consent isn’t a one-time pass for every time. You have bodily autonomy and the right to say no.

Whether you're seeking a committed relationship or looking for the newest addition to the roster, I hope you feel empowered to make the decisions that make the most sense for you.

And if you have to have the dreaded “what are we” conversation, I’m sending all my love.

Until next time,

Miss Behave

title: How to celebrate Juneteenth in State College this weekend
author: Saxtyn Lutz | The Daily Collegian
description:
This weekend, community members will gather in downtown State College to celebrate Juneteenth National Independence Day.

Beginning over two years after the Emancipation Proclamation was issued, Juneteenth commemorates the day enslaved Texans learned they were free, June 19, 1865. A year later, those who were freed in Texas organized an annual celebration, first known as Emancipation Day, which later spread nationwide and became recognized as a federal holiday in 2021.

It is the longest running African American holiday, celebrating Black history, resilience and freedom.

State College’s Juneteenth Celebration Weekend events are hosted by the Borough of State College, the Happy Valley Adventure Bureau and the Center for the Performing Arts at Penn State.

Arts and Crafts Exhibition Opening Reception

Beginning at 5:30 p.m. on Friday, June 19, an opening reception for the Juneteenth Art Exhibition will take place at the Woskob Family Gallery, located inside the Penn State Downtown Theatre Center on South Allen Street.

The event will start with a drum-call opening and processional. Between 7 p.m. and 8 p.m, the reception will close with a panel conversation of six artists. Their work explores freedom, justice and equality.

Artists include Kennedy Ferguson, Barry King, Irvin Moore, June Ramsay, Helen Velásquez Martinez and Aiysha Watson.

Arts and Crafts Exhibition

The exhibition will be held in the Woskob Family Gallery from June 19 to August 7.

Curated by Grace Hampton, the exhibition titled “Freedom, Justice, Equality - An Intergenerational Perspective” uses photography, visual art, sculpture, crafts and more.

The exhibition will be open every Friday from June 19 to August 7 from 12:30 p.m. to 5 p.m.

During the Central Pennsylvania Festival of the Arts, there will be additional hours for viewing on July 8 to July 11 from 9 a.m. to 5 p.m. and July 12 from 11 a.m. to 2 p.m.

Block Party

From noon to 6 p.m. on Saturday, June 20, the Juneteenth Block Party will take place at the Dr. Martin Luther King, Jr. Plaza and 100 Block of South Fraser Street.

The block party will include musicians — including headliner Maya Azucena — spoken word artists, cultural performers, dancers, food trucks and various Black-owned vendors.

The Makery will offer a free Juneteenth Community Paper Weaving Project. Other activities include a scavenger hunt and face painting.

“Music in the Colored Conventions”

A digital exhibit called “Music in the Colored Conventions” was launched by the Center for Black Digital Research.

The Colored Conventions were created in 1830 to bring together Black leaders to discuss injustices faced by free, escaped and enslaved Black people. Singing and performances were regular occurrences during these conventions, showcasing the role of artistic expression in the fight for social reform.

The exhibit highlights those who sang in Colored Conventions and other 19th century musical artists who sustained Black musical traditions that contributed to the movements for Black liberty and freedom.

Virtual Bookshelf

The Penn State University Libraries’ Libby mobile app has a “Celebrate Juneteenth!” virtual bookshelf.

The selection provides books by Black authors, featuring Black stories exploring the history and legacy of emancipation and resistance.

The Libby reading app offers Penn State students and employees free access to leisure reading and audiobooks.

1865 Freedom Legacy Scholarship

In 2024, the Juneteenth planning committee established the 1865 Freedom Legacy Scholarship Fund to increase affordability and access to post-secondary education.

The scholarship recognizes the community service and academic excellence of racially underrepresented and Indigenous students in State College and Bellefonte, and it aims to create cultural awareness and civic engagement at the high-school level. Community members can donate in support of the scholarship.

To access more information about this scholarship visit here.

title: Penn State men's hockey releases 2026-27 schedule
author: Lexie Linderman | Football Editor
description:

Penn State next goes on the road to play Notre Dame before one more game against Robert Morris on Feb. 6.

The Nittany Lions then have two straight home series against Michigan State and Minnesota before travelling to Ohio State to end the February slate.

title: Column | Should Penn State fans be worried about recent recruiting losses?
author: Andrew Deal | The Daily Collegian
description:
After a major official visit weekend to begin June, things have gone south for Penn State’s 2027 class.

All the hype that was garnered from the massive weekend has seemingly disappeared, begging the question — should Penn State fans be worried about these recent recruiting losses?

On Sunday, June 7, Penn State recruiting was in its best position it may be this entire cycle. Nineteen of the 20 commits at the time were on a visit — with safety commit Jonathan Galette scheduled to visit the following week — and the Nittany Lions were gaining major traction on multiple highly regarded recruits.

The biggest were 4-star wide receivers Khalil Taylor and Deshawn Hall, who would’ve added to a wide receiver class that already included 4-stars Jamir Dean and Landon Blum.

Just 24 hours later, it all went downhill. The first commit of the class, 3-star cornerback Semajay Robinson, flipped his commitment to Virginia, and two days later, 3-star cornerback Zachary Gleason Jr. flipped to West Virginia.

Dean flipped to Georgia just days later, Taylor is now projected to go to Nebraska and Hall is predicted to go to Auburn, leaving just Blum as the lone 2027 wide receiver. Additionally, longtime linebacker target Case Alexander is now projected to remain in state and commit to Oklahoma. Just like that, all the hype that was built on Sunday had completely vanished.

But it’s still too early to panic about the upcoming recruiting class. A majority of the coaching staff is just getting its feet wet with the resources Penn State has to offer and it won’t likely be until 2028 that the future of recruiting at Penn State is fully seen.

The losses — if they happen — will definitely hurt the trajectory of the class, and morale around Penn State fans will likely be down. Regardless, where the class is right now without having even seen a game played with this new coaching staff and system is still impressive.

Everything could flip back to the Nittany Lions’ side if they win some of the “tough” games. And while there is validity to panic from fans, the team is sitting at a top 25 class in the 247Sports composite team rankings and has seven 4-stars committed.

However, there is one trend in this recruiting class that could cause some concern. Among the 20 players committed, just three come from Pennsylvania. In a 2027 class that is loaded with in-state talent, the Nittany Lions swinging and missing on a majority of the top talent can be worrisome.

Currently, Penn State has more players committed in New Jersey with five than its own state. While no singular team has dominated the state in the 2027 class, it’s allowed some major programs to enter itself into the Pennsylvania ecosystem.

Defensive line commit Stanley Montgomery ranks No. 6 in the state, but Pennsylvania currently has two 5-stars and at least one player inside the top 10 nationally for the first time since 2020 when Julian Fleming was ranked No. 3.

Both Florida offensive tackle commit Maxwell Hiller and Georgia running back commit Kemon Spell sit inside the top 10, and both decided to leave their home state to play football, just like Fleming did when he signed with Ohio State.

Outside of Montgomery, Penn State has no other players committed inside the top 10 of the Keystone State in the 2027 class, something that’s definitely slightly alarming. Taylor, who’s the No. 4 player from Pennsylvania, could still return to the Nittany Lions, but recent comments from him and predictions make it seem like he’s heading to Nebraska.

On the bright side, Montgomery hails from the Philadelphia area, somewhere James Franklin and the previous staff struggled to recruit in. But without bringing in other highly rated Philadelphia area players, the Nittany Lions may still not be able to pull from the eastern side of the state.

The lack of Pennsylvanians in the class, especially in a time period when the state seems to be open for grabs, isn't good for future years.

No matter how the next few weeks shape out, this time of year is the time of panic or hope for college football fans, with everything being hypotheticals for the season and the future. However, Penn State fans shouldn’t panic and should still feel optimistic about Campbell’s first full class in Happy Valley, even though he’s struggling to recruit in-state.

title: The Lady Lion careers of women’s basketball’s coaching staff
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
After Pat Kraft hired former Lady Lion Tanisha Wright as the next Penn State head coach in March, Wright quickly established a supporting cast of former Lady Lions to help her lead the program.

The Lady Lions have four former players on the coaching staff, and with the help of these Lady Lion legends, there’s an opportunity to rebuild the program.

Here’s a look at each of the four Lady Lion alumni and their historic Penn State careers.

Tanisha Wright (2001-2005)

The seventh head coach in Lady Lion history had a very successful career in Happy Valley, ending as the then-No. 4 leading scorer of all time. While she’s now eighth in that list with 1,995 career points, Wright cemented herself as one of the best defensive players to take the court for Penn State.

Throughout her four years with the Lady Lions, the West Mifflin, Pennsylvania, native helped lead Penn State to four straight NCAA Tournament appearances, including a Sweet Sixteen appearance her junior year and Elite Eight appearance her senior year. Wright started all but eight games during her entire collegiate career, making 27 starts her freshman year and served as a full-time starter for the other three seasons.

Wright was a strong contributor to the team in each of her four years. During her freshman season, she recorded a career-high 20 blocks to go along with 10.1 points and four rebounds per game, followed by 16 points per game and 90 steals in Year 2.

After averaging 14.8 points and 4.7 rebounds per game her junior season, Wright came back and capped off her career with her highest-scoring season yet, recording 19.3 points per game — the then seventh-most points in a single season in school history.

Penn State’s newest head coach earned three of Penn State’s four Big Ten Defensive Player of the Year honors in school history during her sophomore, junior and senior season.

Kelly Mazzante (2000-2004)

Penn State’s all-time leading scorer joined the Lady Lions on April 1 following three seasons coaching at Georgetown.

With the Lady Lions, Mazzante produced the best career in Penn State history, earning a spot atop dozens of school records. After four seasons, Mazzante recorded 2,919 points, which is still 309 points higher than No. 2 in school history.

Mazzante’s standout season came in Year 2 of her collegiate career, where she averaged 24.9 points per game.

Like Wright, Mazzante experienced success in the NCAA Tournament, helping Penn State clinch a spot in each of her four years. The two spent three seasons together, building a 77-27 record.

The Montoursville, Pennsylvania, native earned numerous awards throughout her four years, including being a two-time AP First Team All-American selection, 2001 Big Ten Freshman of the Year, a three-time unanimous Big Ten First Team honoree and two-time Big Ten Player of the Year.

Angie Potthoff (1992-1997)

Also among the top scorers in school history is Penn State’s newest chief of staff, who is currently 11th all-time in scoring.

Potthoff had a slow start to her Lady Lion career, averaging just 4.5 points per game while coming off the bench her entire freshman year. However, things picked up in Year 2 as she made 10 starts, recording 17.8 points per game on the season.

The Erie, Pennsylvania, native continued to climb in her junior year, but made the biggest impact in her final season when she averaged a double-double. Potthoff recorded 19.5 points and 11.1 rebounds per game.

While the Potthoff era experienced the worst overall record between the four coaches with a 15-12 record her senior year, she aided her team to three 20-win seasons and three tournament appearances.

Susan Robinson Fruchtl (1988-1992)

While Mazzante is currently the all-time leading scorer in Penn State history, Robinson Fruchtl held that record for 12 years following her time in Happy Valley.

Robinson Fruchtl led the Lady Lions to their first glimpse of success in her last three years with the program, including a 29-2 season in 1990-91. During her career, Penn State amassed a 92-30 record and earned two NCAA Tournament wins in its three appearances.

The Center Point, West Virginia, native averaged over 18 points per game in each of her four seasons at Penn State and averaged 8.8 rebounds during her career.

Robinson Fruchtl is the only Wade Trophy winner in school history, having earned the honor in 1992.

title: Former Penn State forward Mya Vaslet selected by PWHL Hamilton in 2026 PWHL Draft
author: Andrew Mercer | The Daily Collegian
description:
After spending five years as a Nittany Lion, Mya Vaslet is a professional hockey player.

Vaslet was selected in the sixth round of the 2026 PWHL Draft by PWHL Hamilton as the last former Penn Stater off the board.

Vaslet played a bottom-six center role during her time in Hockey Valley, but maintained consistent scoring marks throughout her collegiate career. She had a pension for showing up in the biggest moments, scoring two opening goals in two of Penn State’s four AHA Championship victories.

In the 2023-24 season, Vaslet’s lone tally put the Nittany Lions past Mercyhurst to capture the team’s second-straight conference title. Two years later, Vaslet opened the scoring to help Penn State take home a 3-2 victory.

However, between those key moments in her career, Vaslet went down with a knee injury just four games into what would’ve been her senior season. She was later taken to the hospital and her season was over. After a lengthy recovery period, Vaslet announced that she’d return for one last dance as a fifth year.

Now, Vaslet will be taking her talent and grit to Hamilton where she’ll likely be fighting for a position as the team’s fourth-line center.

title: Former Penn State defenseman Kendall Butze selected by PWHL Las Vegas in 2026 PWHL Draft
author: Dominic Megats I The Daily Collegian
description:
One of Penn State’s best defensemen is going pro.

Kendall Butze was selected in the fifth round of the 2026 PWHL Draft by PWHL Las Vegas. She was the second defenseman selected from Penn State in this year's draft and the sixth Nittany Lion headed to the next level.

The Cleveland, Ohio, native set several records before departing from Happy Valley. Butze set a new season record for assists by a defenseman with 28 and points by a defenseman with 33. Additionally, she now sits at first in career assists by a defenseman with 79 and points by a defenseman with 94.

Butze closed out her career with Penn State after her best season yet. She finished with a career-high in both goals and assists, ultimately finishing with 33 points.

At the other end of the ice, the defenseman led the team in blocked shots with 53 and posted a plus-minus of 35. She was only penalized twice throughout the season, putting her discipline on display.

As a result of such an historic season, Butze was named AHA Best Defenseman for the second-straight year. She also joined three other Nittany Lions after being named to the All-AHA First Team.

Butze’s playmaking ability was evident throughout the season, ending in the Frozen Four in which she assisted the first goal of the National Semifinal on the power play.

Now, one of the top defensemen in the draft looks to continue to find success in Las Vegas.

title: Penn State forward Katelyn Roberts selected by New York Sirens in 2026 PWHL Draft
author: Myles Hannak | The Daily Collegian
description:
After a decorated four-year career in Happy Valley, Katelyn Roberts is off to the next level.

The forward was selected in Round 4 of the 2026 PWHL Draft by the New York Sirens, marking the fifth blue and white player to be selected so far.

During her four seasons donning the blue and white sweater, Roberts appeared in 153 games with 43 goals and 60 assists over that period. The four-time AHA Tournament winner was named an alternate captain in Penn State’s Frozen Four Run last season, where it fell short.

The Chanhassen, Minn. native also made back-to-back All-AHA teams, first as a member of the First Team in 2024-25 and most recently on the second team in 2025-26.

Roberts is one of six Penn State skaters to log over 100 points in their careers. Now, she takes that prestige to New York with an opportunity to continue her successes.

title: Former Penn State goaltender Katie DeSa selected by Vancouver Goldeneyes in 2026 PWHL Draft
author: Andrew Mercer | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State’s elite goaltender is set to stop pucks in the pros.

Katie DeSa was selected with the 37th overall pick in the fourth round of the 2026 PWHL Draft by the Vancouver Goldeneyes, making her the fourth Nittany Lion drafted.

The Pawcatuck, Connecticut, native closed out her career with a 64-13-3 record. Her 25 career shutouts, 0.818 winning percentage and 1.43 goals-against average and all program records.

She became the second back-to-back AHA Goaltender of the Year in history in her final two years at Penn State. Additionally, she was a two-time finalist for the HCA National Goalie of the Year award.

DeSa was the backup behind Josie Bothun in her freshman season when the Nittany Lions took home their first of four AHA Tournament Championships. DeSa inherited the starting role the next year and didn’t look back, completing her collegiate career as a four-time conference tournament champion.

As a senior, she was a crucial piece to Penn State’s Frozen Four run with a 27-4 record, .936 save percentage and was the NCAA Shutouts Champion with 12. She notably had a 36-save shutout against UConn in the Regional Finals to help send Penn State through to its first-ever Frozen Four.

Now, DeSa will be taking her talents to Vancouver where she’ll likely be the backup behind Emerance Maschmeyer.

MORE HOCKEY COVERAGE

title: Former Penn State forward Maddy Christian selected by Minnesota Frost in 2026 PWHL Draft
author: Dominic Megats I The Daily Collegian
description:
Another talented forward is going pro.

Maddy Christian was selected with the 33rd overall pick in the third round of the 2026 PWHL Draft by the Minnesota Frost, making her the third player to be selected from Penn State.

The Elk River, Minnesota, native is fresh off one of her best seasons in the blue and white. Christian finished third on the team in goals with 19 and third in points with 35.

During her time in Happy Valley, Christian played in 153 games posting 46 goals and 38 assists, finishing with 84 points.

She served as an alternate captain in her final season. The forward contributed throughout the entire season, finishing the Frozen Four run with an assist in the National Semifinal game on an Abby Stonehouse go-ahead goal in the first period.

Now, Christian looks to provide depth in the forward position as she heads to Minnesota.

title: Penn State women's basketball to face Arizona State in 2026-27 season
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
The first opponent in Penn State's 2026-27 season has been announced.

The Lady Lions will travel to Tempe to face Arizona State on Nov. 15, marking their first road matchup against the Sun Devils in school history.

Penn State faced Arizona State in the Bryce Jordan Center last season, losing 74-60 in the first-ever matchup between the two schools.

Arizona State assistant coach Daniel Barber is going into his second season with the Sun Devils following one season at Penn State from 2024-25.

title: Former Penn State defenseman Leah Stecker selected by Boston Fleet in 2026 PWHL Draft
author: Andrew Mercer | The Daily Collegian
description:
Another one of Penn State’s hallmark defensemen is bound for the PWHL.

Leah Stecker was selected with the 27th overall pick in the third round of the 2026 PWHL Draft by the Boston Fleet. She was the second Nittany Lion drafted.

Stecker was a steady four-year contributor under coach Jeff Kampersal and a pillar of the four-time AHA Tournament Championship class alongside many of her professionally-bound classmates. She didn’t miss a single game in her last three seasons, playing in 115-consecutive contests.

She closed out her collegiate career with 12 goals and 45 assists and was named to the All-AHA Tournament team as a junior in 2025. On the other end of the ice, Stecker finished with 83 blocked shots and an impressive 73 plus-minus ratio.

As an alternate captain in her senior campaign, Stecker scored three goals and added 17 assists on offense. On defense, she blocked 32 shots and maintained a plus-minus of 29 on the season.

She helped make history on Jan. 30 when she assisted the first-ever goal at Beaver Stadium, scored by McKenna Walsh. Additionally, she showed up big in the Frozen Four, blocking a career-high five shots against Wisconsin to keep the game close.
