title: Former Penn State center Tessa Janecke selected No. 3 overall by PWHL Las Vegas in 2026 PWHL Draft
author: Andrew Mercer | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State’s all-time greatest hockey player is headed to the biggest stage in professional women’s hockey.

Tessa Janecke was selected with the No. 3 overall pick in the first round of the 2026 PWHL Draft by PWHL Las Vegas. She was the first Nittany Lion to have her name called in this year’s draft and became the highest-selected player in Penn State history.

Janecke was nothing short of a sensation since her arrival in Hockey Valley, having already taken home silver at the 2022 U18 Women's World Championships. She was an instant success in her freshman year as the HCA Rookie of the year and USCHO Co-Rookie of the Year with the 2026 first overall pick Caroline Harvey.

Over the course of Janecke’s career, she set program records in nearly every offensive category including her 201 points, 89 goals, 112 assists and +112 plus-minus ratio among many others.

She was a two-year captain as well as the program’s first top-3 finalist for the Patty Kazmaier Memorial Award for the nation’s top player. She was the sparkplug that led the Nittany Lions to winning four-consecutive AHA Tournament titles and got them to their program-first Frozen Four.

She made school history when she became Penn State’s first Winter Olympic gold medalist with Team USA at the Milano-Cortina 2026 games, racking up five assists in the tournament as the fourth-line center.

In addition to her Olympic success, Janecke was already a two-time IIHF Women’s World Championships gold medalist with the U.S. Women’s National Team. She etched her name into the international history book when she scored the golden goal in the 2025 gold-medal game to beat Team Canada.

Most recently, Janecke was Penn State’s star player in the 2026 Frozen Four against Wisconsin. She scored the opening goal as well as a late game-tying goal to plunge the teams into overtime.

Now, Janecke is headed to the PWHL where she plans to make a splash just as she did at Penn State and on the world stage.

title: Penn State men's basketball lands commitment from St. Bonaventure transfer Dasonte Bowen
author: Will Horstman | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State has made a late splash in the transfer portal on Wednesday.

St. Bonaventure transfer point guard Dasonte Bowen has committed to the Nittany Lions, according to his Instagram page.

Bowen, who was listed at 6-foot-2 and 190 pounds last season, will provide needed depth at point guard for Penn State. He was granted a fifth season of eligibility, as his junior season was cut short due to injury.

Bowen averaged 10.9 points, 3.0 rebounds and 4.8 assists per game with the Bonnies last season, starting all 33 games at point guard for them. He'll likely be a backup behind senior Jay Rodgers but will still see considerable minutes in the rotation.

title: Film review | 4-star EDGE Elijah Guertin brings superior technique, blazing speed to Penn State
author: Andrew Mercer | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State secured its first true edge rusher commit in the Class of 2027 when 4-star Elijah Guertin announced his commitment on Sunday.

Guertin ranks as the No. 1 player out of Rhode Island and No. 27 edge in the class, per 247Sports composite. He helped Bishop Hendricken High School capture the Rhode Island Division I state championship as a junior despite a wrist injury that kept him out for most of the season.

He has also taken his athleticism to the Hawks’ track and field team, showcasing immense speed and mobility for his 6-foot-5, 255-pound frame. Guertin placed third in the 110-meter hurdles at the state championships last year.

Guertin’s film showcases his lightning-fast agility and bodily control. Combine that with a collegiate-level build and Guertin can make an immediate impact when he arrives on campus.

Here’s a closer look at his film.

Lethal run defense

Guertin’s agility and wingspan allow him to quickly shed opponents and reach inside to shut down interior gaps. Running backs will see open lanes and attempt to blast through, but Guertin uses cat-like reflexes to shut the door just in time, only needing one arm to stop the rusher in his tracks.

It starts on the line of scrimmage, as Guertin makes a heavy, targeted strike under his opponent’s arms to establish control. From that position, whether the running back goes inside or outside, Guertin pushes off his matchup and uses the momentum to kickstart his approach.

He possesses the mental quickness to immediately lock onto the ball carrier instead of trying to always collapse the pocket, and he plays a patient but fast-paced rushing game.

Once he gets a hand on the ball carrier, they’re both hitting the turf. Guertin puts his full frame into each tackle, whether he’s wrapping his arms around the opponent’s torso or he’s jumping on their back so they fall forward.

That said, Guertin doesn’t have to make every play on his own. He works in tandem with the rest of the defensive line by forcing the ball carrier into the middle where his teammates are waiting to pounce.

When a rusher does break through, he collaborates with the linebackers’ knee-high tackles by aiming his high to secure the down.

Quarterback hunter

When the quarterback drops back to pass, this is where Guertin’s speed and explosiveness shines brightest. He uses his ability to push away from opponents to break into the pocket, drawing the rest of the offensive line to collapse.

However, Guertin is often too swift and gets close enough to the quarterback to throw him off his game before the opposing line can stop him. And because he’s in the pocket so early, throws are often made before the receivers can get into position.

Using his height, he can get vertical to disrupt passes when the quarterback has enough time to avoid the sack. Sometimes he’ll cause the quarterback to scramble, forcing them to go through their progressions or settle for throwing the ball away.

Regardless, Guertin ensures that he always has a hand in the passing lane and times his attacks well to make deflections.

Guertin also doesn’t give up on the play if his initial burst is turned aside. Guertin displays the tenacity and adaptability required to free himself from his matchup and make a play on a quarterback who’s lingering in the pocket.

If the quarterback decides to take it himself, Guertin turns on the afterburners and hunts them down for a sack or forces an incompletion.

Body control

Guertin isn’t just a fundamentally sound player, he also uses his superior sense of balance and control to make the most out of any play. When he doesn’t have a clear lane through to the pocket, he uses a variety of different counter measures to give himself the advantage, including spin moves.

Guertin doesn’t wait for lanes to open, he actively takes initiative as a craftsman with great vision and urgency in attacking weaknesses. If he’s being pushed too far outside the pocket, a clean spin maneuver gets him right back in place and with the separation he desired.

Whether he’s attacking the inside or outside zones, he can tear upwards on the opponent's guards to force them off balance before he makes a break for the pocket. Couple that with nifty footwork, and it’s hard to keep up with him when he’s on his game.

Guertin doesn’t make unnecessary movements and always appears to have full control with what he wants to do. That allows him to change directions quickly, recover smoothly if needed and continue the fight from any angle.

When he’s on the ground, however, he’s not always out. There have been times where he’ll continue to be a pest by clutching tightly to the opponent's cleats from the turf, slowing them down just enough for his teammates to secure the tackle.

Each move is calculated yet fluid. Guertin has a set plan for each snap and thinks a step ahead in case his original route is shut down, but he isn’t afraid to make decisions on the fly.

title: Column | Penn State's wrestling dominance might not be worth it
author: Myles Hannak | The Daily Collegian
description:
It’s time to rattle the cage in Happy Valley, and Penn State is yet again flexing its athletic muscle and devotion to wrestling.

After a parade of athletic letdowns everywhere but the mat, the university is in a place where results haven’t cut it overall. I recognize some folks will try to spin the “why” of this differently, but there’s still one glaring potential solution staring the university in the face.

If you’ve read my last few columns, it’s pretty obvious. Penn State’s athletic department is a commercial juggernaut, with money pouring into every sport from hockey hype to basketball’s annual postseason disappearing act. Nobody’s even pretending to debate it.

I already crowned Cael Sanderson the most untouchable coach in Happy Valley and the sport’s kingpin. Hoarding national titles in a sport dripping with tradition is impressive, and the investment has built not only a college powerhouse but also a pipeline for America’s best.

But that also means it's comfortable leaving other programs to scrounge for what’s left.

The coach and administration’s approach to the sport is the reason the blue and white’s name carries weight, fills seats and lures top recruits.

This is Penn State, after all. What would it be without wrestling?

Fans and administrators love to point to the trophy case as proof that the plan works. That’s all well and good, but at some point it starts to look like overwatering a plant that’s already outgrown its pot. Pat Kraft bleeds wrestling, as evidenced by his always-occupied mat-side seat and his steady hand to keep the cash flowing. But it’s time to ask whether pouring more into a program already lapping the field still makes sense.

Frankly, it’s getting dull when the only suspense in Vegas’ books is who finishes second, while Penn State’s wrestling bench reads like a who’s who of talent. Instead of piling more gold onto a mountain, why not spread the wealth?

Start with a small tweak of resources and set clear benchmarks for other squads, and review the impact at season’s end; track the results and see if the grass gets greener. I’m not saying I could do better, but I think it’s worth trying for the benefit of other teams.

Shaking up the status quo won’t be easy. The wrestling faithful will dig in, and shifting budgets will mean managing change and bruised egos. Deciding which programs get a boost will ignite debate, but facing these impediments head-on builds trust and shows fans that winning is a goal, not limited to just the mat in Rec Hall.

That’s how you get more banners in rafters across campus, and grow Penn State’s fan base across all athletic corners.

Being a Nittany Lions fan means you’ve probably got a shrine of what happens on the mat. Lately, though, it all feels a little stale. Not the winning, of course. Other sports keep moving forward, while wrestling’s stuck in a cycle of dominance. The efforts to keep it are too immense.

Alums in the sport have given something to cheer about and have helped shape the wider wrestling community, but for a school branded as a wrestling powerhouse, the lack of current pro alumni is noticeable as the old guard begins to slow from competition.

It’s especially crucial today, as Olympic golds matter less to the public and youth. The current most notable former Nittany Lions wrestler is the UFC’s Bo Nickal, and while he embraces those college ties, he’s one of the sport’s most polarizing figures.

Sure, there have been all-time legends. But as the saying goes: What have you done for me lately? The Carter Starocci story is the only recent headline that has lasted, really, and it’s a negative one. It just doesn’t feel like the product has matched its previous level, though that can change with the flick of a light switch, especially with the current rostered talent. I’ll admit that.

Giving the mat a breather and shifting some attention to tennis, lacrosse or basketball can unlock potential and bring new energy to Penn State athletics. The tide always turns, and this dominance may not last.

Frankly, I can’t wait for there to be genuine competition again.

Our former wrestling reporter, Andrew Deal, called it months ago: Penn State isn’t even doing right by wrestling without a women’s program, even though adding one would be a major shift for the so-called home of the sport. And the demand is there, with women’s wrestling exploding at the high school level and the NCAA already designating it an emerging sport.

So, what’s the snag? Sure, the trophies keep piling up and Olympians keep rolling through, but is Penn State really pushing the sport forward? Winning is great, but developing the game matters, too.

The university needs to loosen the reins and let wrestling grow naturally, as it should, instead of funneling the sport’s development through a single room.

Winning and dominance could be achieved without its current approach, and it would mean more if the sport wasn’t just a pencil-in prediction that Penn State wrestlers bring home a national championship every year.
